im apart of a group that has google TRC. so we get our tpu costs waived. now we do NOT get storage costs or networking waived. what i want to do is create the VERY BEST image → location model that exists and make that record stand for a long ass time. our tpu quotas are in the thousands. so yeah. the issue is, how do we deal with storage/netowkring?

Place your Google Cloud Storage buckets in the exact same GCP region as your allocated TPUs. Reading data from a bucket in the same region incurs zero network egress charges. If you read data across regions or from outside GCP, you will pay network fees and create a bottleneck that leaves the TPUs idle waiting for data. 

Downsample all images to the exact resolution your model accepts before uploading them. Storing original high-resolution images wastes space and increases costs. Compress the downsampled images using JPEG and pack them sequentially into TFRecords. Storing heavily compressed, resized images on GCS Standard storage costs roughly $20 to $26 per terabyte per month. Do not use cheaper Coldline or Archive storage tiers for active training data; they charge data retrieval fees and lack the throughput to keep thousands of TPUs fed.

Since TRC only waives TPU costs, you must pay the monthly GCS invoice. TRC participants typically apply for Google Cloud Research Credits to cover these baseline storage costs. If you do not secure these credits, your group will have to pay the storage bill directly. There is no method to bypass GCP storage fees while training on TPUs at this scale, as the data must reside on GCS to achieve necessary read speeds.

Holding an image-to-location benchmark record for an extended period is improbable. Large technology companies possess proprietary location datasets containing billions of images. Your model's accuracy is constrained by the volume of data you can afford to store and process. When an organization trains on a more expansive dataset, they will surpass your benchmark.

Storing 12 TB on GCS Standard costs about $240 to $310 a month. If you format the images to a standard training resolution like 224x224 pixels, 12 TB holds around 500 million to 1 billion images. Make sure the bucket is in the exact same region as your TPUs so you do not generate network transfer fees.

Scraping 4 TB of Street View photos is a breach of the Google Maps Terms of Service, which forbids using their mapping data to train machine learning models. Since you rely on Google's TRC program for compute, doing this puts your TPU allocation at risk. If they identify the scraped data, they can suspend your TRC access and your cloud account.

The other 8 TB must come from public datasets containing user-uploaded photos. User photos rely on device GPS, which is often inaccurate. They also feature varying camera hardware, indoor settings, and inconsistent angles. Combining structured, street-level Street View imagery with unstructured user photos creates a disjointed dataset. The model will need to reconcile the differences between uniform road data and random vacation photos, complicating the training process and limiting accuracy on both formats.

OpenStreetView-5M contains 5.1 million street-level images across 225 countries. It enforces distance separation between training and testing data to prevent models from memorizing locations. The data relies on crowdsourced contributions, meaning camera quality and capture angles fluctuate.
Mapillary Street-Level Sequences provides 1.6 million street-level images from 30 cities. It captures the same locations across different seasons, weather conditions, and times of day. Its geographic scope restricts coverage to a few urban areas, lacking global rural distribution.
The Yahoo Flickr Creative Commons 100 Million (YFCC100M) dataset includes around 48 million geotagged photos. Derived datasets like MP16-Pro filter this to 4.1 million images. Flickr data leans heavily toward tourist landmarks and specific population centers. The GPS metadata is frequently inaccurate due to device errors or manual user tagging.
Google Landmarks v2 provides 5 million images covering 200,000 structures and natural sites. It trains models to recognize specific places but fails for generic street corners, forests, or residential areas.
Cross-view datasets pair ground-level images with satellite overheads. The CORE dataset contains 1 million image pairs from 225 regions. CVSat has 422,000 aerial images and 175,000 street panoramas. Satellite combinations give models an absolute geographic reference. Strict alignment criteria limit the volume of usable training data, and temporal gaps between ground and aerial captures cause scene mismatches.
Indoor locations require distinct datasets since GPS signals drop inside buildings. The NAVER LABS dataset contains 130,000 images from transit stations and malls. The Zillow Indoor Dataset has 71,000 panoramas of unfurnished homes. Indoor data is fragmented, small, and maps to local floor plans rather than global coordinates.





Here is the storage breakdown for the datasets. Uncompressed sizes run larger than the downloaded archives, so you need overhead for processing.
| Dataset | Focus | Image Count | Approximate Storage Size |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **YFCC100M** | Generic crowdsourced | 100 million (99.2M photos) | 13.5 TB (images) / 12.5 GB (metadata) |
| **Google Landmarks v2** | Tourist landmarks | 5 million | 585 GB |
| **OpenStreetView-5M** | Global street-level | 5.1 million | ~500 GB |
| **Mapillary Street-Level Sequences** | Urban street-level | 1.6 million | ~150 GB |
| **CORE** | Ground & aerial pairs | 1 million pairs | ~100 GB |
| **CVSat** | Ground & aerial pairs | 597,694 | ~60 GB |
| **NAVER LABS** | Indoor stations & malls | 130,000 + LiDAR | ~50 GB |
| **Zillow Indoor (ZInD)** | Indoor residential | 71,474 panoramas | 40 GB |
Scraping the actual image files for YFCC100M or dependent subsets like MP16-Pro is a bottleneck. The 13.5 TB figure relies on grabbing the original files directly from Flickr, but millions of those links have died since the 2014 release. You will recover a smaller fraction and use less storage if you pull it today. 
Running models over multiple terabytes of data requires substantial compute and local storage bandwidth. If you merge satellite imagery, street-level data, and indoor panoramas into your training pipeline, you will need a distributed storage setup to handle the frequent read requests without stalling your GPUs.

Drop the indoor datasets and the cross-view satellite datasets. Indoor photos of unfurnished residential rooms or malls lack the visual context needed to determine global coordinates. Satellite datasets rely on matching overhead views with ground-level images, requiring a model architecture that accepts dual inputs for contrastive retrieval. If your model takes a single standard image as input, feeding it top-down aerial imagery or blank interior walls will degrade its accuracy.

YFCC100M is your only realistic option to fill the remaining 8 TB with user-taken photos, but the dead link issue is severe. You will spend weeks running scraping scripts across your gigabit connection only to retrieve a fraction of the original 100 million images. YFCC100M and Google Landmarks v2 also suffer from heavy geographic bias. They contain disproportionate amounts of data from North America, Western Europe, and famous tourist sites. 

If you use the datasets exactly as downloaded, your model will overfit to population centers. If the model is fed significantly more photos of Paris than rural Argentina, it will minimize its training loss by guessing Paris whenever it encounters an ambiguous urban photo. Mapillary presents the exact same problem; its data is restricted strictly to 30 cities, forcing the model to bias its predictions toward those specific locations.

OpenStreetView-5M is the most useful public dataset on your list for this exact task because it enforces spatial distribution across 225 countries. To prevent the massive YFCC100M and Landmarks datasets from overriding your globally distributed data, you must filter your final mixture geographically. You have to limit the maximum number of images allowed per square kilometer before packing the files into TFRecords. Balancing the location distribution limits the total volume of your dataset, meaning you likely will not reach your 12 TB goal, but it will yield a more accurate model.

To achieve maximum accuracy, extract standard field-of-view images from the 4 TB of Street View panoramas. A standard photo captures a narrow angle. Extracting multiple standard crops from a single 360-degree panorama removes the capture vehicle structure and simulates normal camera lenses. This bridges the visual gap between street-level captures and user-uploaded photos.

Base your architecture on a large Vision Transformer initialized with existing image weights. Do not design the network to output raw latitude and longitude coordinates. Direct regression of exact coordinates yields unstable training and severe outlier predictions. Instead, partition the Earth using a spatial indexing system such as S2 geometry or H3 hexagons. Frame the task as a classification problem where the model outputs a probability distribution across these geographic cells.

To utilize your TPU allocation, distribute the dataset to process high batch sizes. High batch sizes stabilize gradient updates when operating over images with extreme geographic variance. Apply image augmentation, including severe cropping, color shifts, and resolution downsampling during training to obscure the differences between standard photography and panoramic capture equipment. 

When processing an image upload, apply multi-crop evaluation. Run multiple variations of the uploaded file through the model, applying different zooms and aspect ratios to each. Average the spatial cell probabilities from these variations. This increases the compute time per user request but provides a more accurate final prediction than a single forward pass.
the streetview dataset is actually 3x images of the same location: left, front, right.

Having pre-rendered directional views removes the need to unwarp 360-degree equirectangular panoramas. This saves preprocessing time and provides a field of view that matches standard cameras. The images are still captured from the center of a road at a fixed height. Apply random cropping during training to remove the bottom section of the frame, which often contains the hood of the capture vehicle or fixed road lines. 

You must group the dataset by coordinates before creating your training and validation splits. If a front view is placed in your training data and the right view of the exact same coordinate is placed in your validation data, you induce data leakage. The model will memorize that specific intersection rather than learning to generalize. All images from a specific coordinate must reside entirely within the training set or entirely within the validation set. Apply geographic distance buffers so validation images are at least several kilometers away from any training image.

Since you have high TPU quotas, use the three views to run a contrastive pretraining phase before training the actual location classifier. Set up a pipeline passing the left, front, and right images of the same location as positive pairs, and images from different locations in the batch as negative pairs. This forces the Vision Transformer to learn the underlying environmental features of a location instead of memorizing specific camera angles. 

When you fine-tune the model for the final prediction, use a hierarchical loss function. Instead of outputting a single probability distribution across thousands of small geographic cells, format the spatial grid to output predictions at multiple scales. Having the model predict the country, the region, and the exact local cell simultaneously allows the network to learn broad geographic indicators. This ensures the loss function scales the penalty, punishing the model severely for guessing the wrong hemisphere, but only slightly for picking an adjacent street.

This teaches the model ecological distributions, helping it identify remote regions where roads and buildings do not exist. Since most social media scrapers pull urban data, biological datasets provide necessary geographic balance.

Hardware sometimes rounds coordinates to a city center, which introduces false labels into your training data. This approach requires two separate data processing pipelines. You cannot use the same TFRecords for both models. Training two separate models doubles your compute time. TRC provides high TPU quotas, but keeping thousands of chips at maximum utilization for weeks across two different training runs might trigger usage limits or prompt Google administrators to manually review your project. A GeoGuessr-specific model needs more than just location coordinates to compete at the highest level. The game involves temporal meta-clues. Specific camera generations, blur boxes, and vehicle antennas only exist in certain countries during specific years. Biasing your dataset toward populated areas matches the distribution of real-world photography. Most users will upload pictures of cities and landmarks. If the model trains on dense data from these locations, it will predict them accurately. The flaw is the model's response to ambiguous images. When fed a photo of an empty dirt road, a biased model will default to predicting a major city because that choice minimizes its statistical loss across the training set. Enforcing a strict global distribution lowers accuracy on specific tourist sites but prevents these severe misclassifications in rural areas. 

Stick to your 12 TB target for the final dataset. To yield 8 TB of usable user-uploaded photos, you must scrape roughly 20 TB of raw files. You will discard a large percentage of the downloads due to missing GPS metadata, indoor settings, formatting errors, and heavy blur. Downsample everything you keep to a uniform training resolution like 224x224 or 256x256 pixels. At those dimensions, 12 TB holds hundreds of millions of images, which is enough volume to fully utilize your TPU quota. 

Google Cloud Standard storage charges $20 to $26 per terabyte per month. A 12 TB dataset costs $240 to $310 every month. If you intend to train both a general model and a GeoGuessr model, your storage requirements will increase. 

You will need the 12 TB processed dataset for the general model, plus the uncropped 4 TB Street View dataset to teach the GeoGuessr model the specific vehicle artifacts. Storing both totals 16 TB, which raises your monthly invoice to $320 to $415. You can keep the tourist photos without deleting them to flatten your distribution. Instead of dropping data, modify the loss function. By weighting the loss according to inverse class frequency, the model receives a severe penalty for misclassifying a rural image and a standard penalty for misclassifying a city photo. This forces the network to learn rural geographic markers while retaining the memorized tourist locations.

Control the data sampling rate during training. Do not feed the TFRecords to the TPUs sequentially. Configure the data pipeline to build batches with a fixed ratio of high-density to low-density locations. This guarantees the network processes obscure coordinates in every training step alongside well-documented landmarks. It stops the model from assuming every ambiguous photo is a major city to minimize baseline loss.

Structure the prediction grid hierarchically. A heavily photographed landmark provides enough data for the model to predict an exact street-level cell. A rural road does not. If the network predicts broad regional partitions alongside small local cells, it outputs the correct state or country even when it fails to pinpoint the exact rural coordinate. This delivers tight precision in cities and broad accuracy in empty regions.

Using these methods means you store the full, unbalanced dataset on GCS. You will not have to discard millions of usable images just to satisfy a geographical cap. It increases the volume of data you must scrape and process, but it maximizes the utility of the parameters you are training.

great idea with the loss. i need you to really think about those ways to solve problems that will come up. you know, i tried to make a geoguessr specific model. here was my training code. thoughts?
```python
import os
import io
import json
import math
import random
import tarfile
import urllib.request
import urllib.error
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Dict, Any, List, Tuple
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import torch.distributed as dist
import torch.multiprocessing as mp
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.utils.data import IterableDataset, DataLoader
from torchvision import models, transforms
from torchvision.models import Swin_V2_B_Weights
import wandb
# ============================================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================================
class Config:
    # Dataset & shard settings
    CACHE_DIR = "./shard_cache"
    MANIFEST_URL = "https://guessrai.tennisbowling.com/dataset/manifest.json"
    TRAIN_RATIO = 0.98  # fraction of shards for training
    # Cursor file for prefetcher coordination
    CURSOR_PATH = "./cursor.json"
    CURSOR_UPDATE_INTERVAL = 50  # Update cursor every N steps
    # Checkpointing
    CHECKPOINT_DIR = "./checkpoints"
    CHECKPOINT_INTERVAL = 1  # Save checkpoint every N epochs
    RESUME_FROM_CHECKPOINT = True  # Auto-resume from latest checkpoint if available
    # Training hyperparameters
    PER_GPU_BATCH = 12  # reduced for DDP (2 GPUs × 12 = 24 global batch)
    NUM_WORKERS = 4     # parallel data loading (can increase to 6-8 if stable)
    NUM_EPOCHS = 100
    WARMUP_EPOCHS = 5
    # Model settings
    CELL_SIZE_KM = 40  # keep grid density
    FEATURE_DIM = 1024
    # Optimizer settings
    BASE_LR = 1e-4
    WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4
    BASE_GLOBAL_BATCH = 16.0  # reference for LR scaling
    # Loss weights
    LAMBDA1 = 1.0  # Level 1 (coarse cell classification) weight
    LAMBDA2 = 10.0  # Level 2 (offset regression) max weight - uses dynamic schedule
    # Logging & evaluation
    LOG_INTERVAL = 100
    MAX_VAL_BATCHES = 200  # cap validation per epoch for speed
    # Weights & Biases
    WANDB_PROJECT = "guessrai"
    WANDB_ENTITY = None  # set to your wandb username/team if needed
    WANDB_RUN_NAME = None  # auto-generated if None
    # Performance toggles
    USE_CHANNELS_LAST = True
    USE_TF32 = True
    # Reproducibility
    SEED = 42
    DETERMINISTIC = False
# ============================================================================
# DISTRIBUTED HELPERS AND SCHEDULER
# ============================================================================
def is_dist_avail_and_initialized() -> bool:
    return dist.is_available() and dist.is_initialized()
def get_rank() -> int:
    return dist.get_rank() if is_dist_avail_and_initialized() else 0
def get_world_size() -> int:
    return dist.get_world_size() if is_dist_avail_and_initialized() else 1
def is_main_process() -> bool:
    return get_rank() == 0
def set_seed(seed: int, deterministic: bool = False):
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    if deterministic:
        torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
        torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
def seed_worker(worker_id):
    worker_seed = torch.initial_seed() % 2**32
    np.random.seed(worker_seed)
    random.seed(worker_seed)
class WarmupCosineScheduler(torch.optim.lr_scheduler.LambdaLR):
    """
    Linear warmup followed by cosine decay (stepped per iteration).
    """
    def __init__(self, optimizer, warmup_steps: int, total_steps: int, last_epoch: int = -1):
        assert total_steps > warmup_steps > 0
        def lr_lambda(current_step: int):
            if current_step < warmup_steps:
                return float(current_step + 1) / float(max(1, warmup_steps))
            progress = (current_step - warmup_steps) / float(max(1, total_steps - warmup_steps))
            return 0.5 * (1.0 + math.cos(math.pi * progress))
        super().__init__(optimizer, lr_lambda, last_epoch=last_epoch)
# ============================================================================
# GRID SYSTEM
# ============================================================================
class GridSystem:
    """Manages the grid covering Earth's inhabited regions."""
    def __init__(self, cell_size_km=20):
        self.cell_size_km = cell_size_km
        self.cell_size_deg = cell_size_km / 111.0
        self.lat_min, self.lat_max = -60, 75
        self.lon_min, self.lon_max = -180, 180
        self.n_lat_cells = int((self.lat_max - self.lat_min) / self.cell_size_deg)
        self.n_lon_cells = int((self.lon_max - self.lon_min) / self.cell_size_deg)
        self.num_cells = self.n_lat_cells * self.n_lon_cells
        if is_main_process():
            print(f"Grid System: {self.n_lat_cells} x {self.n_lon_cells} = {self.num_cells} cells")
            print(f"Cell size: {self.cell_size_km}km (~{self.cell_size_deg:.3f}°)")
    def coord_to_cell(self, lat, lon):
        lat_idx = int((lat - self.lat_min) / self.cell_size_deg)
        lon_idx = int((lon - self.lon_min) / self.cell_size_deg)
        lat_idx = max(0, min(lat_idx, self.n_lat_cells - 1))
        lon_idx = max(0, min(lon_idx, self.n_lon_cells - 1))
        return lat_idx * self.n_lon_cells + lon_idx
    def cell_to_coord(self, cell_idx):
        lat_idx = cell_idx // self.n_lon_cells
        lon_idx = cell_idx % self.n_lon_cells
        lat = self.lat_min + (lat_idx + 0.5) * self.cell_size_deg
        lon = self.lon_min + (lon_idx + 0.5) * self.cell_size_deg
        return lat, lon
    def get_nearby_cells(self, lat, lon, k=20):
        center_cell = self.coord_to_cell(lat, lon)
        center_lat_idx = center_cell // self.n_lon_cells
        center_lon_idx = center_cell % self.n_lon_cells
        radius = int(math.sqrt(k)) + 2
        nearby = []
        for dlat in range(-radius, radius + 1):
            for dlon in range(-radius, radius + 1):
                lat_idx = center_lat_idx + dlat
                lon_idx = center_lon_idx + dlon
                if 0 <= lat_idx < self.n_lat_cells and 0 <= lon_idx < self.n_lon_cells:
                    cell_idx = lat_idx * self.n_lon_cells + lon_idx
                    cell_lat, cell_lon = self.cell_to_coord(cell_idx)
                    dist = haversine_distance(lat, lon, cell_lat, cell_lon)
                    nearby.append((cell_idx, dist))
        nearby.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
        return nearby[:k]
def haversine_distance(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2):
    """Great-circle distance between two points on Earth (in km)."""
    R = 6371.0
    lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2 = map(math.radians, [lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2])
    dlat = lat2 - lat1
    dlon = lon2 - lon1
    a = math.sin(dlat/2)**2 + math.cos(lat1) * math.cos(lat2) * math.sin(dlon/2)**2
    c = 2 * math.asin(math.sqrt(a))
    return R * c
def create_soft_labels(lat, lon, grid_system, k=20, sigma=20.0):
    """
    Create soft labels for Level 1 based on distance to nearby cells.
    Returns:
        indices: Tensor [k] of cell indices
        weights: Tensor [k] of probabilities (sum to 1)
    """
    nearby = grid_system.get_nearby_cells(lat, lon, k)
    indices = [cell_idx for cell_idx, _ in nearby]
    distances = [dist for _, dist in nearby]
    weights = np.exp(-np.array(distances, dtype=np.float32)**2 / (sigma**2))
    weight_sum = weights.sum()
    # Handle edge case where all weights underflow to zero
    if weight_sum > 0:
        weights = weights / weight_sum
    else:
        # Fallback: uniform distribution over k nearest cells
        weights = np.ones(k, dtype=np.float32) / k
    return torch.LongTensor(indices), torch.FloatTensor(weights)
# ============================================================================
# SHARD AND MANIFEST HELPERS
# ============================================================================
def fetch_manifest(manifest_url: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    with urllib.request.urlopen(manifest_url, timeout=30) as r:
        return json.loads(r.read().decode("utf-8"))
def update_cursor_file(cursor_path: str, current_index: int):
    """Write current shard index to cursor file for prefetcher coordination."""
    try:
        with open(cursor_path, 'w') as f:
            json.dump({"current_index": current_index}, f)
    except Exception:
        # Don't crash training if cursor update fails
        pass
def log_cache_stats(cache_dir: str):
    """Log cache statistics: number of shards and total size."""
    try:
        tar_files = [f for f in os.listdir(cache_dir) if f.endswith('.tar')]
        part_files = [f for f in os.listdir(cache_dir) if f.endswith('.part')]
        total_size = 0
        for f in tar_files:
            fpath = os.path.join(cache_dir, f)
            total_size += os.path.getsize(fpath)
        size_gb = total_size / (1024**3)
        print(f"[CACHE STATS] {len(tar_files)} shards ready, {len(part_files)} downloading, {size_gb:.1f} GB total")
    except Exception as e:
        # Don't crash if cache stats fail
        pass
def save_checkpoint(checkpoint_dir: str, epoch: int, global_step: int, model, optimizer, scheduler, best_median: float, is_best: bool = False):
    """Save training checkpoint."""
    os.makedirs(checkpoint_dir, exist_ok=True)
    # Extract model state (handle DDP wrapper)
    from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
    model_state = model.module.state_dict() if isinstance(model, DDP) else model.state_dict()
    checkpoint = {
        'epoch': epoch,
        'global_step': global_step,
        'model_state_dict': model_state,
        'optimizer_state_dict': optimizer.state_dict(),
        'scheduler_state_dict': scheduler.state_dict(),
        'best_median': best_median,
    }
    # Save latest checkpoint
    latest_path = os.path.join(checkpoint_dir, 'checkpoint_latest.pth')
    torch.save(checkpoint, latest_path)
    # Save best checkpoint if this is the best model
    if is_best:
        best_path = os.path.join(checkpoint_dir, 'checkpoint_best.pth')
        torch.save(checkpoint, best_path)
    return latest_path
def load_checkpoint(checkpoint_dir: str, model, optimizer, scheduler):
    """Load training checkpoint if available. Returns (start_epoch, global_step, best_median) or (0, 0, inf) if no checkpoint."""
    latest_path = os.path.join(checkpoint_dir, 'checkpoint_latest.pth')
    if not os.path.exists(latest_path):
        return 0, 0, float('inf')
    try:
        checkpoint = torch.load(latest_path, map_location='cpu')
        # Load model state (handle DDP wrapper)
        from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
        if isinstance(model, DDP):
            model.module.load_state_dict(checkpoint['model_state_dict'])
        else:
            model.load_state_dict(checkpoint['model_state_dict'])
        optimizer.load_state_dict(checkpoint['optimizer_state_dict'])
        scheduler.load_state_dict(checkpoint['scheduler_state_dict'])
        epoch = checkpoint['epoch']
        global_step = checkpoint['global_step']
        best_median = checkpoint.get('best_median', float('inf'))
        if is_main_process():
            print(f"✓ Resumed from checkpoint: epoch {epoch}, step {global_step}, best_median {best_median:.2f}km")
        return epoch, global_step, best_median
    except Exception as e:
        if is_main_process():
            print(f"⚠ Failed to load checkpoint: {e}")
        return 0, 0, float('inf')
def list_local_shards_from_manifest(cache_dir: str, manifest: Dict[str, Any]) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
    shards = []
    for s in manifest.get("shards", []):
        name = s["name"]
        size = int(s.get("bytes", 0))
        samples = int(s.get("samples", 0))
        local_path = os.path.join(cache_dir, name)
        if os.path.isfile(local_path):
            try:
                st = os.stat(local_path)
                if size == 0 or st.st_size == size:
                    shards.append({"name": name, "path": local_path, "bytes": st.st_size, "samples": samples})
            except FileNotFoundError:
                pass
    shards.sort(key=lambda x: x["name"])
    return shards
def split_train_val_shards(local_shards: List[Dict[str, Any]], train_ratio: float = 0.98):
    n = len(local_shards)
    split = int(n * train_ratio)
    return local_shards[:split], local_shards[split:]
def assign_shards_for_rank(shards: List[Dict[str, Any]], rank: int, world_size: int) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
    # Disjoint assignment of shard files per DDP rank
    return shards[rank::world_size]
def _group_key_and_view(name: str):
    """
    Given a tar member name, returns (group_key, view_key, ext)
    Expects files like "<id>.front.webp" / "<id>.left.webp" / "<id>.right.webp" / "<id>.json"
    Also supports "<id>.view0.webp"/view1/view2 as fallback.
    """
    base = os.path.basename(name)
    parts = base.split(".")
    if len(parts) < 2:
        return None, None, None
    group_key = parts[0]
    if parts[-1].lower() == "json":
        return group_key, "json", "json"
    view_key = parts[1].lower() if len(parts) >= 3 else None
    ext = parts[-1].lower()
    return group_key, view_key, ext
# ============================================================================
# ITERABLE DATASET WITH SHARD CYCLING AND EPOCH SHUFFLE
# ============================================================================
class ShardedStreetViewDataset(IterableDataset):
    """
    IterableDataset that streams samples from a list of local tar shards.
    For training, it can cycle through its shards indefinitely and reshuffle per epoch.
    Each sample in a shard must have:
      - three images with suffixes: front/left/right (preferred), or view0/view1/view2
      - one JSON with {"lat": float, "lon": float}
    """
    def __init__(self, shards: List[Dict[str, Any]], grid_system: GridSystem, transform=None,
                 shuffle: bool = True, cycle: bool = False):
        super().__init__()
        self.shards = shards  # per-rank list
        self.grid_system = grid_system
        self.transform = transform or transforms.Compose([
            transforms.Resize((224, 224)),
            transforms.ToTensor(),
            transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                                 std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
        ])
        self.shuffle = shuffle
        self.cycle = cycle
        self._epoch = 0
        self.current_shard_index = 0  # Track current shard for cursor updates
    def set_epoch(self, epoch: int):
        self._epoch = int(epoch)
    def _iter_tar(self, tar_path: str):
        """
        Iterate samples from a single tar file.
        Yields dict with keys: 'views' (tensor [3,3,H,W]), 'cell_indices', 'cell_weights', 'offset', 'true_coords'
        """
        groups: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]] = defaultdict(dict)
        # Log when we start reading a new shard
        shard_name = os.path.basename(tar_path)
        if is_main_process():
            import time
            if not hasattr(self, '_last_shard_log_time'):
                self._last_shard_log_time = time.time()
                self._shards_consumed = 0
            self._shards_consumed += 1
            elapsed = time.time() - self._last_shard_log_time
            if elapsed > 300:  # Log every 5 minutes
                rate = self._shards_consumed / (elapsed / 3600.0)  # shards per hour
                print(f"[SHARD STATS] Consumed {self._shards_consumed} shards in {elapsed/60:.1f}min (rate: {rate:.2f} shards/hour)")
                self._last_shard_log_time = time.time()
                self._shards_consumed = 0
        try:
            with tarfile.open(tar_path, mode="r") as tf:
                for member in tf:
                    if not member.isfile():
                        continue
                    gk, vk, ext = _group_key_and_view(member.name)
                    if gk is None or vk is None:
                        continue
                    fobj = tf.extractfile(member)
                    if fobj is None:
                        continue
                    # Always close the extracted file object
                    with fobj:
                        if vk == "json":
                            try:
                                meta = json.loads(fobj.read().decode("utf-8"))
                                groups[gk]["meta"] = meta
                            except Exception:
                                continue
                        else:
                            try:
                                data = fobj.read()
                                img = Image.open(io.BytesIO(data)).convert("RGB")
                                img = self.transform(img)
                                groups[gk][vk] = img
                            except Exception:
                                continue
                    # Try to assemble a sample
                    g = groups[gk]
                    have_meta = ("meta" in g) and ("lat" in g["meta"]) and ("lon" in g["meta"])
                    have_views_named = all(k in g for k in ("front", "left", "right"))
                    have_views_indexed = all(k in g for k in ("view0", "view1", "view2"))
                    if have_meta and (have_views_named or have_views_indexed):
                        if have_views_named:
                            ordered = [g["front"], g["left"], g["right"]]
                        else:
                            # reorder (front, left, right) ≈ (view0, view2, view1)
                            ordered = [g["view0"], g["view2"], g["view1"]]
                        views = torch.stack(ordered)  # [3, 3, H, W]
                        lat = float(g["meta"]["lat"])
                        lon = float(g["meta"]["lon"])
                        # Level 1 soft labels
                        indices, weights = create_soft_labels(lat, lon, self.grid_system)
                        # Level 2 offsets (use cell latitude for conversion consistency with eval)
                        true_cell = self.grid_system.coord_to_cell(lat, lon)
                        cell_lat, cell_lon = self.grid_system.cell_to_coord(true_cell)
                        off_lat = (lat - cell_lat) * 111.0
                        # Use cos(cell_lat) here to match eval conversion exactly
                        off_lon = (lon - cell_lon) * 111.0 * math.cos(math.radians(cell_lat))
                        offset = torch.FloatTensor([off_lat, off_lon])
                        yield {
                            "views": views,
                            "cell_indices": indices,
                            "cell_weights": weights,
                            "offset": offset,
                            "true_coords": torch.FloatTensor([lat, lon]),
                        }
                        # free memory for group after yield
                        del groups[gk]
        except Exception as e:
            if is_main_process():
                print(f"Warning: failed reading tar {tar_path}: {e}")
    def __iter__(self):
        # Split shards across dataloader workers
        worker_info = torch.utils.data.get_worker_info()
        if worker_info is None:
            worker_id = 0
            num_workers = 1
        else:
            worker_id = worker_info.id
            num_workers = worker_info.num_workers
        # Per-epoch deterministic shuffle of shard order for this rank
        shard_order = list(self.shards)
        if self.shuffle:
            rnd = random.Random(self._epoch + 17)  # fixed offset to vary from global SEED
            rnd.shuffle(shard_order)
        # Distribute shards across workers
        worker_shards = shard_order[worker_id::num_workers]
        # Iterate shards once (validation) or cycle indefinitely (training)
        if not self.cycle:
            for shard in worker_shards:
                tar_path = shard["path"]
                if (not os.path.isfile(tar_path)) or tar_path.endswith(".part"):
                    continue
                for sample in self._iter_tar(tar_path):
                    yield sample
        else:
            # Cycle through worker_shards indefinitely
            idx = 0
            n = len(worker_shards)
            if n == 0:
                return
            while True:
                shard = worker_shards[idx]
                tar_path = shard["path"]
                # Update current shard index (extract from filename: dataset-XXXXXX.tar)
                try:
                    shard_name = os.path.basename(tar_path)
                    if shard_name.startswith("dataset-") and shard_name.endswith(".tar"):
                        self.current_shard_index = int(shard_name[8:14])  # Extract XXXXXX
                except:
                    pass
                # Check if shard is available
                if not os.path.isfile(tar_path):
                    if is_main_process():
                        print(f"[WARNING] Shard not yet downloaded: {shard_name} - Training waiting for prefetcher!")
                    import time
                    time.sleep(5)  # Wait 5 seconds and retry
                    continue
                elif tar_path.endswith(".part"):
                    if is_main_process():
                        print(f"[WARNING] Shard still downloading: {shard_name} - Training waiting!")
                    import time
                    time.sleep(5)  # Wait 5 seconds and retry
                    continue
                if os.path.isfile(tar_path) and not tar_path.endswith(".part"):
                    for sample in self._iter_tar(tar_path):
                        yield sample
                idx = (idx + 1) % n
                # On wrap-around, reshuffle within worker to vary shard sequence further
                if idx == 0 and self.shuffle:
                    rnd = random.Random(self._epoch + 31 + worker_id)  # worker-specific
                    rnd.shuffle(worker_shards)
# ============================================================================
# MODEL ARCHITECTURE
# ============================================================================
class CrossViewAttention(nn.Module):
    """Transformer to fuse information across 3 views."""
    def __init__(self, dim=1024, num_heads=8, num_layers=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.pos_embeddings = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(3, dim))
        encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            d_model=dim, nhead=num_heads, dim_feedforward=4096, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=num_layers)
    def forward(self, features):
        # features: [B, 3, dim]
        features = features + self.pos_embeddings.unsqueeze(0)  # [B, 3, dim]
        attended = self.transformer(features)                   # [B, 3, dim]
        fused = attended.mean(dim=1)                            # [B, dim]
        return fused
class GeolocalizationModel(nn.Module):
    """Two-level hierarchical geolocation model."""
    def __init__(self, num_cells, feature_dim=1024):
        super().__init__()
        # Swin V2-B backbone
        swin = models.swin_v2_b(weights=Swin_V2_B_Weights.DEFAULT)
        swin.head = nn.Identity()  # output features instead of logits
        self.backbone = swin
        backbone_out_dim = 1024  # Swin V2-B feature dimension
        # Project to feature_dim if needed
        self.feature_proj = nn.Identity() if backbone_out_dim == feature_dim else nn.Linear(backbone_out_dim, feature_dim)
        self.cross_view_attention = CrossViewAttention(dim=feature_dim)
        # Level 1: classifier head (keep grid density unchanged)
        self.level1_head = nn.Linear(feature_dim, num_cells)
        # Level 2: Offset regression head
        self.level2_head = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(feature_dim, 512),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.3),
            nn.Linear(512, 256),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.3),
            nn.Linear(256, 2),
            nn.Tanh()
        )
        # Predict offsets in [-max_offset_km, +max_offset_km]
        self.max_offset_km = 20.0
    def forward(self, views):
        """
        Args:
            views: [B, 3, 3, H, W]
        Returns:
            level1_logits: [B, num_cells]
            level2_offset: [B, 2] - (Δlat_km, Δlon_km)
        """
        B = views.shape[0]
        # Flatten views: [B*3, 3, H, W]
        views_flat = views.view(B * 3, views.shape[2], views.shape[3], views.shape[4])
        # Apply channels_last to 4D tensor for performance (only valid for 4D)
        if views_flat.is_cuda:
            views_flat = views_flat.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)
        # No gradient checkpointing: we have VRAM on 5090s
        features = self.backbone(views_flat)         # [B*3, 1024]
        features = self.feature_proj(features)       # [B*3, feature_dim]
        features = features.view(B, 3, -1)          # [B, 3, feature_dim]
        fused = self.cross_view_attention(features)  # [B, feature_dim]
        level1_logits = self.level1_head(fused)      # [B, num_cells]
        level2_offset = self.level2_head(fused)      # [B, 2] in [-1, 1]
        level2_offset = level2_offset * self.max_offset_km
        return level1_logits, level2_offset
# ============================================================================
# LOSS FUNCTIONS
# ============================================================================
def soft_cross_entropy_loss(logits, indices, weights):
    """
    Soft cross-entropy using k target indices with corresponding weights.
    logits: [B, C], indices: [B, k], weights: [B, k]
    Compute log-softmax in float32 for numerical stability with large C.
    """
    log_probs = F.log_softmax(logits.float(), dim=1)           # [B, C], float32
    gathered = torch.gather(log_probs, 1, indices)             # [B, k]
    loss = -(weights * gathered).sum(dim=1).mean()
    return loss
def offset_loss(pred_offset, true_offset):
    return F.mse_loss(pred_offset, true_offset)
def get_lambda2(epoch, warmup_epochs=5, max_lambda2=10.0):
    """
    Dynamic lambda2 schedule:
    - During warmup: linearly ramp from 0.1 to 1.0
    - After warmup: exponentially increase (triples every 2 epochs) up to max_lambda2
    This prevents Level 2 offset regression from oscillating wildly before
    Level 1 has learned which cell/continent the image is in.
    """
    if epoch < warmup_epochs:
        return 0.1 + (1.0 - 0.1) * (epoch / warmup_epochs)
    else:
        t = epoch - warmup_epochs
        return min(1.0 * (3.0 ** (t / 2)), max_lambda2)  # triples every 2 epochs
# ============================================================================
# CUDA STREAM PREFETCHER
# ============================================================================
class AsyncBatchPrefetcher:
    """Prefetches batches using CUDA streams for overlapped transfer and compute."""
    def __init__(self, dataloader, device, use_channels_last: bool):
        self.dataloader = dataloader
        self.device = device
        self.use_channels_last = use_channels_last
        self.stream = torch.cuda.Stream()
        self.next_batch = None
    def __iter__(self):
        self.iter = iter(self.dataloader)
        self._preload()
        return self
    def _preload(self):
        try:
            batch = next(self.iter)
        except StopIteration:
            self.next_batch = None
            return
        with torch.cuda.stream(self.stream):
            views = batch['views'].to(self.device, non_blocking=True)
            if self.use_channels_last and views.ndim == 4:
                views = views.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)
            self.next_batch = {
                'views': views,
                'cell_indices': batch['cell_indices'].to(self.device, non_blocking=True),
                'cell_weights': batch['cell_weights'].to(self.device, non_blocking=True),
                'offset': batch['offset'].to(self.device, non_blocking=True),
                'true_coords': batch['true_coords']  # keep on CPU for eval
            }
    def __next__(self):
        torch.cuda.current_stream().wait_stream(self.stream)
        batch = self.next_batch
        if batch is None:
            raise StopIteration
        self._preload()
        return batch
# ============================================================================
# TRAIN / EVAL (DDP + BF16)
# ============================================================================
def train_epoch(model, dataloader, optimizer, device, scheduler, max_batches: int,
                lambda1=1.0, max_lambda2=10.0, log_interval=100, use_channels_last=True, epoch=0, global_step=0,
                dataset=None, cursor_path=None, cursor_interval=50, cache_dir=None, warmup_epochs=5):
    """
    One training epoch with BF16 autocast and per-iteration scheduler.
    Runs for exactly max_batches per rank to keep DDP in lockstep.
    Returns: dict with losses and the final global_step counter.
    """
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0
    total_level1_loss = 0.0
    total_level2_loss = 0.0
    num_steps_local = 0
    # Compute dynamic lambda2 based on epoch
    lambda2 = get_lambda2(epoch, warmup_epochs, max_lambda2)
    if is_main_process():
        print(f"  Using lambda2={lambda2:.4f} for epoch {epoch} (max={max_lambda2})")
    prefetcher = AsyncBatchPrefetcher(dataloader, device, use_channels_last)
    for batch_idx, batch in enumerate(prefetcher):
        if batch_idx >= max_batches:
            break
        views = batch['views']                # [B, 3, 3, H, W]
        cell_indices = batch['cell_indices']  # [B, k]
        cell_weights = batch['cell_weights']  # [B, k]
        true_offset = batch['offset']         # [B, 2]
        optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
        with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
            level1_logits, level2_offset = model(views)
            loss_l1 = soft_cross_entropy_loss(level1_logits, cell_indices, cell_weights)
            loss_l2 = offset_loss(level2_offset, true_offset)
            loss = lambda1 * loss_l1 + lambda2 * loss_l2
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        if scheduler is not None:
            scheduler.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
        total_level1_loss += loss_l1.item()
        total_level2_loss += loss_l2.item()
        num_steps_local += 1
        global_step += 1
        if is_main_process() and (batch_idx + 1) % log_interval == 0:
            current_lr = optimizer.param_groups[0]['lr']
            print(f"  Batch {batch_idx+1}/{max_batches}: "
                  f"Loss={loss.item():.4f}, L1={loss_l1.item():.4f}, L2={loss_l2.item():.4f}, LR={current_lr:.6e}")
            # Log to wandb
            wandb.log({
                'train/loss': loss.item(),
                'train/loss_level1': loss_l1.item(),
                'train/loss_level2': loss_l2.item(),
                'train/learning_rate': current_lr,
                'train/lambda2': lambda2,
                'train/epoch': epoch,
            }, step=global_step)
        # Update cursor file for prefetcher coordination
        if is_main_process() and cursor_path and dataset and (batch_idx + 1) % cursor_interval == 0:
            current_shard = getattr(dataset, 'current_shard_index', 0)
            update_cursor_file(cursor_path, current_shard)
            # Log cache stats every 10 cursor updates (every 500 steps by default)
            if cache_dir and (batch_idx + 1) % (cursor_interval * 10) == 0:
                log_cache_stats(cache_dir)
    # Global average across ranks
    sums = torch.tensor([total_loss, total_level1_loss, total_level2_loss, float(num_steps_local)],
                        dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    if is_dist_avail_and_initialized():
        dist.all_reduce(sums, op=dist.ReduceOp.SUM)
    denom = max(1.0, sums[3].item())
    return {
        'total': (sums[0].item() / denom),
        'level1': (sums[1].item() / denom),
        'level2': (sums[2].item() / denom),
        'global_step': global_step,
    }
@torch.no_grad()
def evaluate(model, dataloader, device, grid_system, max_batches: int = None, use_channels_last=True):
    """
    Distributed-aware evaluation. Each rank evaluates its shard subset.
    If max_batches is provided, stop after that many batches per-rank.
    """
    model.eval()
    distances_local: List[float] = []
    num_batches = 0
    with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
        for batch in dataloader:
            if max_batches is not None and num_batches >= max_batches:
                break
            views = batch['views'].to(device, non_blocking=True)
            if use_channels_last and views.ndim == 4:
                views = views.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)
            true_coords = batch['true_coords']  # [B, 2] on CPU
            level1_logits, level2_offset = model(views)
            pred_cells = level1_logits.argmax(dim=1).cpu()  # [B]
            pred_offsets = level2_offset.cpu()              # [B, 2]
            for i in range(pred_cells.shape[0]):
                cell_lat, cell_lon = grid_system.cell_to_coord(pred_cells[i].item())
                off_lat_km, off_lon_km = float(pred_offsets[i, 0]), float(pred_offsets[i, 1])
                pred_lat = cell_lat + off_lat_km / 111.0
                # Convert with cos(cell_lat) to match training offset definition
                pred_lon = cell_lon + off_lon_km / (111.0 * math.cos(math.radians(cell_lat)))
                true_lat, true_lon = float(true_coords[i, 0]), float(true_coords[i, 1])
                dist_km = haversine_distance(pred_lat, pred_lon, true_lat, true_lon)
                distances_local.append(dist_km)
            num_batches += 1
    # Gather distances across ranks
    if is_dist_avail_and_initialized():
        local_t = torch.tensor(distances_local, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
        local_len = torch.tensor([local_t.numel()], dtype=torch.int64, device=device)
        lengths = [torch.zeros_like(local_len) for _ in range(get_world_size())]
        dist.all_gather(lengths, local_len)
        lengths = [l.item() for l in lengths]
        max_len = max(lengths) if lengths else 0
        if local_t.numel() < max_len:
            pad = torch.zeros(max_len - local_t.numel(), dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
            local_t = torch.cat([local_t, pad], dim=0)
        gathered = [torch.zeros(max_len, dtype=torch.float32, device=device) for _ in range(get_world_size())]
        dist.all_gather(gathered, local_t)
        if is_main_process():
            all_distances = []
            for g, L in zip(gathered, lengths):
                all_distances.extend(g[:L].cpu().numpy().tolist())
            distances = np.array(all_distances, dtype=np.float32)
        else:
            distances = None
    else:
        distances = np.array(distances_local, dtype=np.float32)
    if not is_main_process():
        return None
    assert distances is not None and distances.size > 0, "No distances collected for evaluation."
    return {
        'median_km': float(np.median(distances)),
        'mean_km': float(np.mean(distances)),
        'acc_1km': float((distances < 1).mean() * 100.0),
        'acc_25km': float((distances < 25).mean() * 100.0),
        'acc_200km': float((distances < 200).mean() * 100.0),
        'acc_750km': float((distances < 750).mean() * 100.0),
        'acc_2500km': float((distances < 2500).mean() * 100.0),
    }

def main():
    # DDP setup
    local_rank = int(os.environ.get("LOCAL_RANK", "0"))
    rank = int(os.environ.get("RANK", "0"))
    world_size = int(os.environ.get("WORLD_SIZE", "1"))
    use_ddp = world_size > 1
    if use_ddp:
        dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl", init_method="env://")
        torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)
    device = torch.device(f"cuda:{local_rank}")
    if is_main_process():
        print(f"Using device: {device} | world_size={world_size} rank={rank} local_rank={local_rank}")
    # Backend performance
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True
    if Config.USE_TF32:
        torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True
        torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32 = True
        # PyTorch 2.0+ matrix precision hint (safe even if not present)
        try:
            torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('high')
        except Exception:
            pass
    # Reproducibility
    set_seed(Config.SEED + rank, deterministic=Config.DETERMINISTIC)
    # Grid system
    grid_system = GridSystem(cell_size_km=Config.CELL_SIZE_KM)
    # Read manifest and discover local shards
    if is_main_process():
        print(f"Reading manifest from {Config.MANIFEST_URL}")
    manifest = fetch_manifest(Config.MANIFEST_URL)
    local_shards_all = list_local_shards_from_manifest(Config.CACHE_DIR, manifest)
    if is_main_process():
        print(f"Found {len(local_shards_all)} local shards in cache_dir={Config.CACHE_DIR}")
    train_shards_all, val_shards_all = split_train_val_shards(local_shards_all, train_ratio=Config.TRAIN_RATIO)
    # Per-rank shard assignment (disjoint)
    train_shards_rank = assign_shards_for_rank(train_shards_all, rank, world_size)
    val_shards_rank = assign_shards_for_rank(val_shards_all, rank, world_size)
    # Compute safe steps_per_epoch as the global minimum across ranks (based on manifest sample counts)
    samples_rank = sum(int(s.get("samples", 0)) for s in train_shards_rank)
    steps_rank = samples_rank // Config.PER_GPU_BATCH
    t_steps = torch.tensor([steps_rank], dtype=torch.int64, device=device)
    if is_dist_avail_and_initialized():
        gathered = [torch.zeros_like(t_steps) for _ in range(world_size)]
        dist.all_gather(gathered, t_steps)
        steps_per_epoch = int(min(g.item() for g in gathered))
    else:
        steps_per_epoch = int(steps_rank)
    steps_per_epoch = max(1, steps_per_epoch)
    if is_main_process():
        print(f"Train shards: total={len(train_shards_all)}, this-rank={len(train_shards_rank)}")
        print(f"Val shards:   total={len(val_shards_all)}, this-rank={len(val_shards_rank)}")
        print(f"Computed steps_per_epoch (global min across ranks): {steps_per_epoch}")
    # Datasets
    common_transform = transforms.Compose([
        transforms.Resize((224, 224)),
        transforms.ToTensor(),
        transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                             std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
    ])
    # Training dataset: cycle and shuffle per epoch
    train_dataset = ShardedStreetViewDataset(
        train_shards_rank, grid_system, transform=common_transform, shuffle=True, cycle=True
    )
    # Validation dataset: no cycle, no shuffle
    val_dataset = ShardedStreetViewDataset(
        val_shards_rank, grid_system, transform=common_transform, shuffle=False, cycle=False
    )
    # Set multiprocessing sharing strategy and context (Python 3.12 compatibility)
    mp.set_sharing_strategy("file_descriptor")
    mp_ctx = mp.get_context("spawn")  # more robust than fork on Py3.12
    # DataLoaders (IterableDataset: no sampler, no shuffle)
    # Reduced workers and disabled pin_memory to avoid IPC/shared-memory pressure
    num_workers = min(4, Config.NUM_WORKERS)
    loader_kwargs = {
        'batch_size': Config.PER_GPU_BATCH,
        'shuffle': False,
        'num_workers': num_workers,
        'pin_memory': False,
        'worker_init_fn': seed_worker,
        'drop_last': False,
    }
    # Only set these if num_workers > 0
    if num_workers > 0:
        loader_kwargs['persistent_workers'] = True
        loader_kwargs['prefetch_factor'] = 2
        loader_kwargs['multiprocessing_context'] = mp_ctx
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, **loader_kwargs)
    val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, **loader_kwargs)
    # Model
    model = GeolocalizationModel(num_cells=grid_system.num_cells, feature_dim=Config.FEATURE_DIM).to(device)
    if Config.USE_CHANNELS_LAST:
        # Apply channels-last memory format (helps on convs/patch embedding)
        model = model.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)
    if use_ddp:
        model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank], output_device=local_rank, find_unused_parameters=False)
    # Optimizer with LR scaling for larger global batch
    global_batch = float(Config.PER_GPU_BATCH * world_size)
    scaled_lr = Config.BASE_LR * (global_batch / Config.BASE_GLOBAL_BATCH)
    if is_main_process():
        print(f"Global batch: {global_batch} (per-GPU {Config.PER_GPU_BATCH} x world_size {world_size})")
        print(f"LR scaling: base_lr={Config.BASE_LR:.2e} -> scaled_lr={scaled_lr:.2e}")
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=scaled_lr, weight_decay=Config.WEIGHT_DECAY)
    # Scheduler: warmup + cosine per-iteration
    total_steps = max(1, steps_per_epoch * Config.NUM_EPOCHS)
    warmup_steps = max(1, steps_per_epoch * Config.WARMUP_EPOCHS)
    scheduler = WarmupCosineScheduler(optimizer, warmup_steps=warmup_steps, total_steps=total_steps)
    # Load checkpoint if resuming
    start_epoch = 0
    global_step = 0
    best_median = float('inf')
    if Config.RESUME_FROM_CHECKPOINT and is_main_process():
        start_epoch, global_step, best_median = load_checkpoint(Config.CHECKPOINT_DIR, model, optimizer, scheduler)
    # Sync checkpoint state across ranks in DDP
    if is_dist_avail_and_initialized():
        checkpoint_state = torch.tensor([start_epoch, global_step], dtype=torch.int64, device=device)
        dist.broadcast(checkpoint_state, src=0)
        if not is_main_process():
            start_epoch, global_step = int(checkpoint_state[0].item()), int(checkpoint_state[1].item())
    # Initialize wandb (rank 0 only)
    if is_main_process():
        wandb.init(
            project=Config.WANDB_PROJECT,
            entity=Config.WANDB_ENTITY,
            name=Config.WANDB_RUN_NAME,
            config={
                'cell_size_km': Config.CELL_SIZE_KM,
                'feature_dim': Config.FEATURE_DIM,
                'per_gpu_batch': Config.PER_GPU_BATCH,
                'global_batch': global_batch,
                'num_workers': Config.NUM_WORKERS,
                'num_epochs': Config.NUM_EPOCHS,
                'warmup_epochs': Config.WARMUP_EPOCHS,
                'base_lr': Config.BASE_LR,
                'scaled_lr': scaled_lr,
                'weight_decay': Config.WEIGHT_DECAY,
                'lambda1': Config.LAMBDA1,
                'lambda2': Config.LAMBDA2,
                'world_size': world_size,
                'steps_per_epoch': steps_per_epoch,
                'use_channels_last': Config.USE_CHANNELS_LAST,
                'use_tf32': Config.USE_TF32,
                'seed': Config.SEED,
                'resumed_from_epoch': start_epoch,
            }
        )
        # Watch model gradients and parameters
        wandb.watch(model, log='all', log_freq=Config.LOG_INTERVAL)
    # Training loop
    for epoch in range(start_epoch, Config.NUM_EPOCHS):
        # Set epoch for dataset shuffling and cycling behavior
        train_dataset.set_epoch(epoch)
        val_dataset.set_epoch(epoch)
        if is_main_process():
            print(f"\n{'='*60}")
            print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{Config.NUM_EPOCHS}")
            print(f"{'='*60}")
            # Log cache status at epoch start
            log_cache_stats(Config.CACHE_DIR)
        train_losses = train_epoch(
            model=model, dataloader=train_loader, optimizer=optimizer, device=device, scheduler=scheduler,
            max_batches=steps_per_epoch, lambda1=Config.LAMBDA1, max_lambda2=Config.LAMBDA2,
            log_interval=Config.LOG_INTERVAL, use_channels_last=Config.USE_CHANNELS_LAST,
            epoch=epoch, global_step=global_step,
            dataset=train_dataset, cursor_path=Config.CURSOR_PATH, cursor_interval=Config.CURSOR_UPDATE_INTERVAL,
            cache_dir=Config.CACHE_DIR, warmup_epochs=Config.WARMUP_EPOCHS
        )
        global_step = train_losses['global_step']
        if is_main_process():
            print(f"Train - Total: {train_losses['total']:.4f}, "
                  f"L1: {train_losses['level1']:.4f}, "
                  f"L2: {train_losses['level2']:.4f}")
            # Log epoch-level training metrics to wandb
            wandb.log({
                'train/epoch_loss': train_losses['total'],
                'train/epoch_loss_level1': train_losses['level1'],
                'train/epoch_loss_level2': train_losses['level2'],
                'epoch': epoch,
            }, step=global_step)
        # Evaluate (distributed), bounded batches
        val_metrics = evaluate(
            model, val_loader, device, grid_system, max_batches=Config.MAX_VAL_BATCHES,
            use_channels_last=Config.USE_CHANNELS_LAST
        )
        if is_main_process() and val_metrics is not None:
            print(f"Val - Median: {val_metrics['median_km']:.2f}km, "
                  f"Mean: {val_metrics['mean_km']:.2f}km")
            print(f"      Acc@1km: {val_metrics['acc_1km']:.1f}%, "
                  f"Acc@25km: {val_metrics['acc_25km']:.1f}%, "
                  f"Acc@200km: {val_metrics['acc_200km']:.1f}%, "
                  f"Acc@750km: {val_metrics['acc_750km']:.1f}%, "
                  f"Acc@2500km: {val_metrics['acc_2500km']:.1f}%")
            # Log validation metrics to wandb
            wandb.log({
                'val/median_km': val_metrics['median_km'],
                'val/mean_km': val_metrics['mean_km'],
                'val/acc_1km': val_metrics['acc_1km'],
                'val/acc_25km': val_metrics['acc_25km'],
                'val/acc_200km': val_metrics['acc_200km'],
                'val/acc_750km': val_metrics['acc_750km'],
                'val/acc_2500km': val_metrics['acc_2500km'],
                'epoch': epoch,
            }, step=global_step)
            if val_metrics['median_km'] < best_median:
                best_median = val_metrics['median_km']
                state = {
                    'epoch': epoch,
                    'model_state_dict': model.module.state_dict() if isinstance(model, DDP) else model.state_dict(),
                    'optimizer_state_dict': optimizer.state_dict(),
                    'median_km': best_median,
                    'config': {
                        'per_gpu_batch': Config.PER_GPU_BATCH,
                        'world_size': world_size,
                        'scaled_lr': scaled_lr,
                        'warmup_epochs': Config.WARMUP_EPOCHS,
                        'num_epochs': Config.NUM_EPOCHS,
                        'steps_per_epoch': steps_per_epoch,
                        'use_channels_last': Config.USE_CHANNELS_LAST,
                        'use_tf32': Config.USE_TF32,
                    }
                }
                torch.save(state, 'best_model.pth')
                print(f"✓ Saved new best model (median: {best_median:.2f}km)")
                # Save model to wandb
                wandb.save('best_model.pth')
                wandb.log({'best_median_km': best_median}, step=global_step)
                # Save best checkpoint
                save_checkpoint(Config.CHECKPOINT_DIR, epoch + 1, global_step, model, optimizer, scheduler, best_median, is_best=True)
        # Save regular checkpoint every N epochs
        if is_main_process() and (epoch + 1) % Config.CHECKPOINT_INTERVAL == 0:
            save_checkpoint(Config.CHECKPOINT_DIR, epoch + 1, global_step, model, optimizer, scheduler, best_median, is_best=False)
            print(f"✓ Saved checkpoint at epoch {epoch + 1}")
        if is_dist_avail_and_initialized():
            dist.barrier()
    if is_main_process():
        print("\nTraining complete!")
        wandb.finish()
    if is_dist_avail_and_initialized():
        dist.destroy_process_group()
if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

how much better would the eva be vs internvit?

InternViT will outperform EVA-CLIP-18B for geolocation in populated areas, while EVA will likely have a slight edge in empty rural landscapes. 

Geolocation relies heavily on text. If an image contains a storefront, a street sign, or a billboard, the fastest way to pinpoint the location is by reading the language, vocabulary, and alphabet. InternViT was explicitly trained on OCR datasets. It knows how to read small, background text across multiple languages. EVA-CLIP-18B relies entirely on web captions. Web captions rarely transcribe background street signs. While EVA is massive, it evaluates text by recognizing blurry pixel patterns rather than actually reading the words. For any user-uploaded photo in a city or town, InternViT will yield a more accurate prediction.

Where EVA-CLIP-18B excels is in raw spatial memorization. With 17.5 billion parameters in the vision tower, it possesses three times the capacity of InternViT. If you feed it a photo of an empty dirt road in rural Mongolia with no text, EVA has the parameter depth to memorize the exact soil color, vegetation distribution, and mountain ridge lines. InternViT has less capacity to store purely geographic noise. 

The hardware reality makes EVA-CLIP-18B a severe liability. Processing high-resolution rectangular images through 17.5 billion parameters generates massive activation tensors. The memory requirements scale quadratically. Even using Fully Sharded Data Parallelism across thousands of TPUs, your per-device batch size will be forced down to 1. Training a global location model with a micro-batch size of 1 destabilizes the gradients because the model cannot average the loss across different continents during a single step.

EVA also amplifies your financial and operational bottlenecks. A 17.5 billion parameter model alongside its optimizer states creates checkpoint files exceeding 200 gigabytes. TRC TPUs preempt constantly. Every time Google reclaims your pod, you must write that 200-gigabyte file to Google Cloud Storage, and then read it back across the network when the pod provisions again. This guarantees a high GCS monthly storage bill and wastes days of compute time just moving files over the network. 

InternViT-6B provides the OCR capabilities required to map human-centric photos, runs at a stable batch size, and keeps checkpoint sizes manageable. Tripling the parameter count with EVA does not compensate for losing the ability to read text and train stably.

InternViT is 45 layers at width 3200; EVA is 48 layers at width 5120. The bf16 weight files alone are about 11.1 GB vs 35.3 GB, so EVA is about \(3.16\times\) heavier before you even count activations.
For training-state memory, a useful planning number is bf16 weights + bf16 grads + fp32 Adam moments, which is about 12 bytes per parameter before sharding. That gives:
| FSDP shards | InternViT training state | EVA training state |
|---|---:|---:|
| 8-way | ~8.3 GB/device | ~26.3 GB/device |
| 16-way | ~4.2 GB/device | ~13.1 GB/device |
That’s before activations and before any all-gather spikes from FSDP. If your stack also keeps fp32 master weights, add another third. For activations, one hidden-stream tensor per layer at batch 1 is about 12.5 MiB/layer for InternViT and 20.0 MiB/layer for EVA, so the lower-bound residual-stream stack is about 0.56 GiB vs 0.94 GiB. Real peaks are higher once you include MLP/attention intermediates and temporary buffers. The practical result is simple: EVA will usually force a smaller microbatch, often by about 2–4x, and on 32 GiB TPU v4 chips it gets tight fast. InternViT is much easier to fit.

- InternViT: about 55 GB without master weights, about 78 GB with them.
- EVA vision encoder: about 175 GB without master weights, about 245 GB with them.

Real GCS writes are slower than that because of sharding, serialization, and object-store overhead. Keep the bucket in the same region as the TPU.

# Model Card for DINOv3

DINOv3 is a family of versatile vision foundation models that outperforms the specialized state of the art across a broad range of settings, without fine-tuning. DINOv3 produces high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models.

## Model Details

These are Vision Transformer and ConvNeXt models trained following the method described in the DINOv3 paper. 12 models are provided:

10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset)

- 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch,
- 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B,
- 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B,

2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset)

- 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch
- 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B

Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens (and register tokens). These models follow a ViT architecture, with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens (for DINOv2 with registers this resulted in 1 + 4 + 256 = 261 tokens).

The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not verified, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size.

### Model Description

- Developed by: Meta AI
- Model type: Vision Transformer, ConvNeXt
- License: DINOv3 License

### Model Sources

- Repository: https://github.com/facebookresearch/dinov3
- Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10104

## Uses

The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks.

### Direct Use

The models can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results:

- on image classification, using k-NN classifiers on the class token
- on image classification, with logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token
- on image classification, with a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens
- on image retrieval using nearest neighbors
- on geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondances
- on depth estimation, semantic segmentation, using linear layers
- on unsupervised object discovery
- on video segmentation tracking
- on video classification, using a small 4-layer attentive probe

### Downstream Use

While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort: the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box.

## Bias, Risks, and Limitations

Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket.

DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2. However, a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa.

### Recommendations

Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels.

## Training Details

### Training Data

Web dataset (LVD-1689M): a curated dataset of 1,689 millions of images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billions web images collected from public posts on Instagram

Satellite dataset (SAT-493M): a dataset of 493 millions of 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution

### Training Procedure

Training objective:

DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop

iBOT masked-image modeling loss

KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens

Gram anchoring

Training regime: PyTorch FSDP2 (with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications)

Distillation:

- Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B.

## Cutting-edge image representations, trained without human supervision

We scaled unsupervised training to 7B-parameter models and 1.7B image datasets, using a fraction of compute compared to weakly-supervised methods. Despite keeping backbones frozen during evaluation, they achieve absolute state-of-the-art performance across diverse domains.

### Exceptional performance across visual domains

SSL unlocks domains where annotations are scarce or costly. Backbones enable state-of-the-art results for tasks including object detection in web imagery, but also canopy height mapping in satellite and aerial imagery.

### Versatile backbone with powerful dense image features

High-resolution dense features from a single DINOv3 backbone enable leading performance across vision tasks, including object detection, depth estimation, and segmentation, without any finetuning.

### Efficient model sizes and architectures

We release a comprehensive model suite addressing a wide range of use cases, including broad coverage of ViT sizes and efficient ConvNeXt models for on-device deployment.

PERFORMANCE

## Evaluating DINOv3's Performance

DINOv3 sets a new standard in vision foundation models. For the first time, a model trained with SSL outperforms weakly-supervised models on a broad range of probing tasks, from fine-grained image classification, to semantic segmentation, to object tracking in video.

APPROACH

## Self-supervised pre-training unlocks simple task adaptation

Pre-training data is curated from a large unlabeled dataset. During pre-training, the model learns general-purpose visual representations, matching features between different augmented views of the same image. In post-training, the model is distilled into more efficient models.

A pre-trained DINOv3 model can be easily tailored by training a lightweight adapter on a small amount of annotated data.

# InternViT-6B-448px-V2_5

[[📂 GitHub]] [[📜 InternVL 1.0]] [[📜 InternVL 1.5]] [[📜 Mini-InternVL]] [[📜 InternVL 2.5]]

[[🆕 Blog]] [[🗨️ Chat Demo]] [[🤗 HF Demo]] [🚀 Quick Start] [[📖 Documents]]

## Introduction

We are excited to announce the release of`InternViT-6B-448px-V2_5`, a significant enhancement built on the foundation of`InternViT-6B-448px-V1-5`. By employing ViT incremental learning with NTP loss (Stage 1.5), the vision encoder has improved its ability to extract visual features, enabling it to capture more comprehensive information. This improvement is particularly noticeable in domains that are underrepresented in large-scale web datasets such as LAION-5B, including multilingual OCR data and mathematical charts, among others.

## InternViT 2.5 Family

In the following table, we provide an overview of the InternViT 2.5 series.

| Model Name | HF Link |
| --- | --- |
| InternViT-300M-448px-V2_5 | 🤗 link |
| InternViT-6B-448px-V2_5 | 🤗 link |

## Model Architecture

As shown in the following figure, InternVL 2.5 retains the same model architecture as its predecessors, InternVL 1.5 and 2.0, following the \"ViT-MLP-LLM\" paradigm. In this new version, we integrate a newly incrementally pre-trained InternViT with various pre-trained LLMs, including InternLM 2.5 and Qwen 2.5, using a randomly initialized MLP projector.

As in the previous version, we applied a pixel unshuffle operation, reducing the number of visual tokens to one-quarter of the original. Besides, we adopted a similar dynamic resolution strategy as InternVL 1.5, dividing images into tiles of 448×448 pixels. The key difference, starting from InternVL 2.0, is that we additionally introduced support for multi-image and video data.

## Training Strategy

### Dynamic High-Resolution for Multimodal Data

In InternVL 2.0 and 2.5, we extend the dynamic high-resolution training approach, enhancing its capabilities to handle multi-image and video datasets.

For single-image datasets, the total number of tiles`n_max` are allocated to a single image for maximum resolution. Visual tokens are enclosed in` ` and` ` tags.

For multi-image datasets, the total number of tiles`n_max` are distributed across all images in a sample. Each image is labeled with auxiliary tags like`Image-1` and enclosed in` ` and` ` tags.

For videos, each frame is resized to 448×448. Frames are labeled with tags like`Frame-1` and enclosed in` ` and` ` tags, similar to images.

### Single Model Training Pipeline

The training pipeline for a single model in InternVL 2.5 is structured across three stages, designed to enhance the model's visual perception and multimodal capabilities.

Stage 1: MLP Warmup. In this stage, only the MLP projector is trained while the vision encoder and language model are frozen. A dynamic high-resolution training strategy is applied for better performance, despite increased cost. This phase ensures robust cross-modal alignment and prepares the model for stable multimodal training.

Stage 1.5: ViT Incremental Learning (Optional). This stage allows incremental training of the vision encoder and MLP projector using the same data as Stage 1. It enhances the encoder’s ability to handle rare domains like multilingual OCR and mathematical charts. Once trained, the encoder can be reused across LLMs without retraining, making this stage optional unless new domains are introduced.

Stage 2: Full Model Instruction Tuning. The entire model is trained on high-quality multimodal instruction datasets. Strict data quality controls are enforced to prevent degradation of the LLM, as noisy data can cause issues like repetitive or incorrect outputs. After this stage, the training process is complete.

## Evaluation on Vision Capability

We present a comprehensive evaluation of the vision encoder’s performance across various domains and tasks. The evaluation is divided into two key categories: (1) image classification, representing global-view semantic quality, and (2) semantic segmentation, capturing local-view semantic quality. This approach allows us to assess the representation quality of InternViT across its successive version updates. Please refer to our technical report for more details.

## Image Classification

Image classification performance across different versions of InternViT. We use IN-1K for training and evaluate on the IN-1K validation set as well as multiple ImageNet variants, including IN-ReaL, IN-V2, IN-A, IN-R, and IN-Sketch. Results are reported for both linear probing and attention pooling probing methods, with average accuracy for each method. ∆ represents the performance gap between attention pooling probing and linear probing, where a larger ∆ suggests a shift from learning simple linear features to capturing more complex, nonlinear semantic representations.

## Semantic Segmentation Performance

Semantic segmentation performance across different versions of InternViT. The models are evaluated on ADE20K and COCO-Stuff-164K using three configurations: linear probing, head tuning, and full tuning. The table shows the mIoU scores for each configuration and their averages. ∆1 represents the gap between head tuning and linear probing, while ∆2 shows the gap between full tuning and linear probing. A larger ∆ value indicates a shift from simple linear features to more complex, nonlinear representations.
