The developers of InternViT-6B-V2.5 explicitly state on their model card that the V2.5 series is engineered for building MLLMs, not for traditional computer vision tasks. The features are optimized to be fed directly into a language model like Qwen or InternLM for next-token prediction. It also enforces the dynamic tiling mechanism we already rejected. If you strip away the language model and try to map its output directly to a classification grid, you are fighting against its pretraining objective. InternViT V1.2 remains the superior choice if you want that specific model family.

DINOv3 ViT-7B completely solves your aspect ratio and hardware bottleneck problems, but it introduces a massive tradeoff regarding text recognition.

On the hardware and architecture side, DINOv3 is practically perfect for your TPU pod. It uses 2D Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) instead of absolute positional embeddings. RoPE strictly calculates relative distances between patches dynamically. Since your target rectangular resolution (like 896x448) consists of dimensions that are exact multiples of the model's patch size (16), DINOv3 natively accepts the wide image out of the box. You do not need to run bicubic interpolation scripts, write padding logic, or mess with dynamic tiling. You just feed it the static 896x448 batch, and XLA will compile it flawlessly. At 6.7 billion parameters, it hits your scaling target and distributes perfectly over FSDP.

The flaw is its pretraining objective. DINOv3 is self-supervised. It was trained on 1.7 billion images using masked-image modeling and self-distillation, entirely without text. It has never seen an image caption, it was not taught to read, and it has zero explicit OCR curriculum. 

Because of this, DINOv3 understands the world purely through visual structure. It can differentiate between the specific bark texture of a tree in Brazil versus one in Colombia better than any contrastive model. But when it sees a street sign, it does not read the words; it just recognizes the geometric shapes of the letters. Your fine-tuning phase will have to map those visual letter shapes to your H3 geographic cells by brute force using your coordinate labels. 

If you want the absolute best performance in rural landscapes, forests, and architecture, DINOv3 will obliterate the competition because its dense feature extraction is unmatched. If you care more about accurately identifying cities by reading storefronts, billboards, and street signs natively, InternViT-6B V1.2 is the better play. You have to decide if you want a model that reads language (InternViT) or a model that excels at raw geographic and spatial features (DINOv3).

Combining InternViT-6B and DINOv3-7B into a dual-backbone architecture gives you the absolute ceiling for accuracy. InternViT extracts the human-centric text, languages, and storefronts. DINOv3 extracts the ecological patterns, weather, and structural geometry. You pass the image through both parallel towers simultaneously and fuse their output tokens. 

Because DINOv3 uses a 16x16 patch size and InternViT uses a 14x14 patch size, they will output different sequence lengths for the exact same image. You resolve this by adding linear projection layers to the end of both backbones to normalize their hidden dimensions to a shared size, such as 4096. You then concatenate the two token sequences into one massive sequence and feed it through a randomly initialized Transformer Encoder block. This fusion block runs self-attention across the combined sequence, allowing the network to mathematically correlate the Thai text found by InternViT with the specific tropical foliage identified by DINOv3. 

To handle the varying image formats on TPUs without triggering XLA compilation stalls, you must use a universal padded canvas. Since compute waste is not a concern, you do not need to crop or distort any images. You define a massive static tensor, for example, 1344x448 pixels. Every single sample you feed the model will be exactly this size.

When processing the Street View data, you concatenate the 448x448 left, front, and right images horizontally. This perfectly fills the 1344x448 canvas. The model processes the full 270-degree panorama exactly as it appears. 

When processing a standard landscape or portrait user photo, you place the image in the dead center of the 1344x448 canvas. The unused space on the left and right sides of the canvas is filled with pure black pixels. You generate a binary attention mask for this specific batch, marking the real image pixels as 1 and the black padding as 0. 

You pass this mask directly into the self-attention layers of the backbones. The models will skip the padding during their attention calculations. This strategy wastes billions of floating-point operations executing matrix multiplications on black pixels, but it perfectly preserves the native aspect ratio and resolution of every single photo in your dataset. Because the outer tensor shape never changes from 1344x448, the XLA compiler remains static, and the TPU pod runs without interruption.

Running an ensemble of 12.5 billion parameters with large sequence lengths requires heavy FSDP sharding across your TPU pod. You will shard the weights of DINOv3, InternViT, and your fusion block. The forward pass pushes the static canvas through both massive networks, fuses them, and routes the final pooled token into your hierarchical S2 classification head to predict the coordinate. This configuration ignores efficiency completely to maximize feature extraction from every pixel of your 12 TB dataset.

You are exactly right. That was a critical oversight. If you stitch the three views together, you create a massive domain gap.

A stitched panorama provides a 270-degree field of view. A standard user photo taken on a phone provides roughly a 60 to 90-degree field of view. If you train the model on stitched panoramas, it will learn to rely on having omnidirectional context. It will expect to see the building in front of it and the street signs on both of its sides simultaneously to make a prediction. 

When a user uploads a single photo of just the building, the model will suddenly lose two-thirds of the visual context it was trained to expect. Its accuracy will collapse because it overfit to the panoramic structure rather than learning to identify locations from a single perspective.

To build a model that dominates on user-uploaded photos, you must completely dismantle the Street View panoramas. 

Treat the left, front, and right images as three entirely independent data points. They will share the exact same coordinate label, but the model will never see them at the same time. By forcing the model to guess the location using only the left image, and then later forcing it to guess using only the right image, you train it to succeed on the exact constrained field of view that a normal phone camera captures. 

Because you are no longer stitching the images horizontally, drop the 1344x448 ultra-wide canvas. Change your universal padded canvas to a massive, high-resolution square, such as 896x896 or 1024x1024. 

When you load a single Street View camera crop (which is usually 4:3) or a user's landscape/portrait photo, you scale it up so its longest edge hits the 1024-pixel boundary. You place it in the center of the canvas and fill the remaining empty space with pure black padding. You generate the attention mask to tell both DINOv3 and InternViT to ignore the black pixels. 

This approach normalizes your massive dataset. Every single image passed to the TPUs—whether it originated from a Google car or a user's iPhone—mimics the exact framing of a standard photograph. 

By splitting the 4 TB of Street View panoramas into roughly 300 million independent training images, you drastically increase your dataset volume. You still process these images through your 12.5 billion parameter combined DINOv3 and InternViT architecture to extract both the OCR and the structural geometry. The TPUs maintain 100% utilization because the tensor remains a static 1024x1024 square, but the model learns strict, single-frame geographic generalization rather than relying on an artificial panoramic crutch.

A standard linear layer mapping two different hidden dimensions to a shared unified dimension does compress the representations. If you force InternViT's 3200-dimensional embeddings and DINOv3's 4096-dimensional embeddings through a single bottleneck before they interact, you permanently alter the raw extraction data.

To prevent any data destruction, use unpooled, bi-directional cross-attention. Cross-attention natively handles differing sequence lengths and hidden dimensions without requiring you to truncate or compress the incoming tensors. 

InternViT outputs 5,329 tokens at dimension 3200. DINOv3 outputs 4,096 tokens at dimension 4096. 

Set up two parallel cross-attention blocks. In the first block, feed DINOv3's 4,096 tokens as the Queries, and InternViT's 5,329 tokens as the Keys and Values. The attention mechanism's internal weight matrices map them to a shared mathematical space solely to compute the attention scores. It then outputs a sequence of exactly 4,096 tokens at dimension 4096. This sequence represents DINOv3's terrain and structural mapping infused directly with InternViT's OCR and text data.

In the second block, do the exact reverse. Feed InternViT's 5,329 tokens as the Queries, and DINOv3's 4,096 tokens as the Keys and Values. This outputs a sequence of exactly 5,329 tokens at dimension 3200, representing the text and human-centric features enriched with DINO's ecological context. 

Neither tensor has been downsampled, pooled, or structurally bottlenecked. You retain 100% of the token count and spatial granularity from both vision encoders. 

You then flatten or concatenate these two enriched sequences along the token dimension to create one massive array of 9,425 tokens. Pass this uncompressed, fully fused sequence into your Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3 spatial correlation layers. The final hierarchical layers will use the full weight of both architectures to map the coordinates without losing any of the native resolution.

The cross-attention block you just designed already performs this exact dynamic weighting natively on a per-image, per-pixel basis. Attention is a mathematical relevance score. The network calculates how important a specific token is to solving the geographic coordinate. 

If you feed the model a picture of an empty forest, InternViT will extract useless noise because there is no text to read. The network will automatically route the gradient updates and the forward pass predictions through DINOv3's structural data because that pathway minimizes the training loss.

Conversely, if you feed the model a storefront in Tokyo, InternViT will output highly confident tokens identifying Japanese characters. Flattening 9,425 tokens representing 3200 and 4096 dimensions creates a single tensor containing over 33 million values per image. If you connect a 33-million dimension vector directly to a classification head predicting 500,000 geographic cells, the resulting linear layer alone contains nearly 17 trillion parameters. Your TPU pod will crash attempting to allocate the gradients for this single layer, regardless of your hardware quota. 

You must compress the token sequences into a dense representation using Attention Pooling before classification. Introduce a set of learned geographic query tokens, for example, 256 tokens initialized randomly. These queries attend to the entire 9,425-token concatenated output of your cross-attention blocks. The queries extract the relevant location features and compress the massive sequence down into a strict 256-token representation. Flatten these 256 tokens and pass them through a standard Multi-Layer Perceptron to create your final continuous feature vector.

Route this feature vector into three separate linear classification heads mapped to an S2 geometry grid. S2 subdivides the sphere into a strict quadtree hierarchy naturally. 

The Level 1 classification head maps the feature vector to roughly 5,000 coarse S2 cells, representing continents and large oceans. The Level 2 head maps the identical vector to roughly 100,000 regional cells, representing states or small countries. The Level 3 head maps it to roughly 1,000,000 fine cells, representing exact city blocks and rural expanses. 

You must apply distance-based label smoothing to the loss function across all three levels. If you use standard hard cross-entropy, the mathematical penalty for guessing a cell 10 meters away from the actual location is identical to the penalty for guessing a cell on the opposite side of the planet. Hard categorical labels assume discrete, unrelated classes, which destroys spatial logic.

Instead of assigning the correct target cell a probability of 1.0 and all other cells a probability of 0.0, you apply a Gaussian blur to the target label based on physical Haversine distance. The exact correct S2 cell receives a target probability of 0.8. The immediately adjacent cells receive 0.15, and the next outer ring receives 0.05. This forces the gradients to teach the model that missing the exact storefront by one street still represents an inherently correct geographic understanding.

During each training step, calculate the smoothed cross-entropy loss for all three levels simultaneously and sum them. This produces a single, combined loss value. The network scales backpropagation perfectly. If the model guesses the wrong continent, all three heads generate a massive loss. If the model guesses the correct continent and state but misses the exact city block, the Level 1 and Level 2 heads generate zero loss, while the Level 3 head generates a small penalty. This stabilizes the training process across the 12.5 billion parameters without stalling the gradient updates.

> Flattening 9,425 tokens representing 3200 and 4096 dimensions creates a single tensor containing over 33 million values per image
what?? how? im kinda lost

Tokens are just lists of numbers. 

DINOv3 breaks your 1024x1024 image into 4,096 patches. The transformer turns each patch into a token with a dimension of 4096. Multiplying 4,096 tokens by 4,096 dimensions gives you about 16.7 million individual numbers representing the structural geometry.

InternViT uses smaller patches, so it chops the same 1024x1024 image into 5,329 tokens. Each token has a dimension of 3200. Multiplying 5,329 by 3200 gives you roughly 17.1 million numbers representing the text and human-centric features.

Combine them, and a single image is represented by a tensor containing 33.8 million numbers. 

If you flatten that tensor—meaning you unroll all those tokens end-to-end to feed them into a classifier—your input is a 1D vector of 33.8 million numbers. 

If your final Level 3 spatial grid has 500,000 geographic cells, the classifier needs a weight connecting every single input number to every single output class. Multiplying 33.8 million inputs by 500,000 outputs gives you 16.9 trillion parameters for that one linear layer. That instantly causes an out-of-memory error and crashes your TPU pod.

This is why you cannot just flatten the sequence output of two massive Vision Transformers. You must pool the tokens to compress them before classification.

Instead of unrolling the 9,425 tokens, you use attention pooling. You create a single trainable summary token, often called a learned query. You write a final attention layer where this single query token looks at the entire sequence of 9,425 tokens. The query extracts the relevant text information from the InternViT tokens and the relevant terrain information from the DINOv3 tokens, and compresses it all into one dense vector. 

If that single summary vector has a dimension of 4096, your input to the classifier is just 4096 numbers instead of 33.8 million. Connecting 4096 inputs to 500,000 geographic cells requires a weight matrix of about 2 billion parameters. That fits easily onto your hardware while preserving the exact geographic features extracted by the 12.5 billion parameter encoder ensemble.

You are right to question that. 4096 is a bottleneck. I was still anchoring slightly to standard memory constraints, but if you truly have terabytes of VRAM and unconstrained TPU pods, compressing the features down to 4096 is a mistake.

DINOv3 natively outputs features at a 4096 dimension. InternViT natively outputs at a 3200 dimension. If you combine them and then force the final result back down to a single 4096-dimensional vector, you are mathematically throwing away nearly half the coordinate width. You choke the 12.5 billion parameter ensemble at the very last step.

To prevent information loss, your pooled vector needs to be larger than the sum of both backbones. You should target a final feature vector of 16,384 dimensions.

Instead of extracting the sequence with a single learned query token, use Multi-Query Attention Pooling (often called a Perceiver Resampler). Initialize 4 independent learned query tokens, each with a dimension of 4096. 

Let all 4 query tokens attend to the massive 9,425-token cross-attention sequence simultaneously. They will naturally specialize during training. One query might focus entirely on text processing, another on structural architecture, another on foliage, and the last on global weather/lighting. 

Take those 4 resulting 4096-dimensional tokens, flatten them end-to-end, and you get one massive 16,384-dimensional feature vector per image. This vector retains the absolute maximum amount of feature richness extracted from the 1024x1024 image.

When you pass this 16,384-dimensional vector into your Level 3 classification head for 500,000 geographic cells, the weight matrix will be exactly 8.1 billion parameters. 

Add that to your Level 1 head, Level 2 head, and the 12.5 billion parameters in your dual vision backbones, and your total architecture sits right around 21 billion parameters. Your instinct is entirely correct. The contrastive coordinate encoder has a fatal mathematical flaw that ruins extreme precision: the Viewpoint Paradox. 

A coordinate is just a point in space. If you stand at an intersection in New York and take a photo facing north, you see skyscrapers. If you face south, you might see a park. Those are two completely different images with different text and structures. The contrastive loss function forces the model to map *both* of those distinct visual vectors into the *exact same* coordinate vector. Because the coordinate network has to satisfy both inputs simultaneously, it averages them out. This forces the coordinate embeddings to become a blurry, generalized representation of the entire 360-degree area, completely destroying the strict street-level precision you want.

If you have infinite compute, terabytes of VRAM, and you want millimeter precision without the limitations of a classification grid or a flawed contrastive MLP, you treat the entire globe as a language and use an **Autoregressive Spatial Decoder**.

Instead of classification or regression, you frame geolocation as sequence generation. Google’s S2 geometry works by projecting the Earth onto a cube with 6 faces. Each face is subdivided into 4 quadrants. Each of those quadrants is subdivided into 4 smaller quadrants, recursively. Any location on Earth, down to the millimeter, can be expressed as a string of numbers. For example, `Face 4 -> Quad 3 -> Quad 1 -> Quad 0` zooms you from the Pacific hemisphere directly onto a specific street corner.

You take your massive 12.5 billion parameter vision ensemble. It outputs the dense sequence of uncompressed tokens. Then, you connect it directly to a 10 to 15 billion parameter Transformer Decoder—essentially the exact same architecture as a large language model like LLaMA or GPT, but uniquely trained to only output S2 quadtree paths.

The massive Transformer Decoder uses cross-attention to look at your vision tokens. It then predicts the location sequentially, token by token. 
Step 1: It predicts the S2 Face (the continent). 
Step 2: Conditioned on that continent, it predicts the next quadrant (the region). 
Step 3: Conditioned on the region, it predicts the next quadrant (the city). 
You let it generate 20 or 30 tokens in a row, zooming in mathematically until you reach sub-meter accuracy.

This architecture completely eliminates the flaws of both previous approaches. 

First, there are zero dead parameters. In a dense classification layer, you waste billions of weights on empty ocean cells. With an autoregressive decoder, the output vocabulary is strictly the numbers 0 through 5. Because the classification head is so tiny, you can reallocate all those parameters into the deep transformer layers. You pack 15 billion weights into the self-attention mechanism, giving the network the raw parameter depth required to meticulously map the geographic relationships of the entire planet. 

Second, it yields infinite precision without inflating the output layer. If you want more precision, you just ask the decoder to predict the next token in the sequence. It logically narrows the physical space by a factor of 4 with every single inference step.

Third, it perfectly handles visual ambiguity. Since it generates the location sequentially, you use Beam Search during inference. If the network sees an ambiguous pine forest, it does not mathematically panic and split the difference into the Atlantic Ocean. Beam Search keeps the top probability paths active. It will generate an S2 path for the Canadian forest and a distinct S2 path for the Russian forest simultaneously, evaluating both down to the millimeter until the sequence ends, delivering two distinct, highly confident coordinate predictions. 

You process perfectly formatted, non-distorted images through 12.5 billion vision parameters natively extracting both text and terrain, fused via cross-attention, and decoded by a 15 billion parameter autoregressive spatial transformer predicting recursive millimeters using Beam Search. That is how you set an unbreakable geolocation benchmark.

You identified the exact mathematical flaw that breaks autoregressive spatial generation. It is called the spatial discontinuity problem. 

Google’s S2 geometry uses a space-filling Hilbert curve to map the sphere. While it tries to keep nearby areas close together in the numerical sequence, it inevitably creates fault lines. Two street corners could be physical neighbors, separated by ten meters of asphalt, but if a major S2 grid boundary runs exactly down the center of that street, their target string sequences will diverge completely right from the first number. 

If you use an autoregressive decoder, the model predicts the sequence one number at a time. If it looks at an image taken exactly on that fault line and hallucinates the first number, it is mathematically trapped. It is forced down a branch of the quadtree that represents a completely different hemisphere. Even with 15 billion parameters, forcing a network to memorize the arbitrary fault lines of a Google quadtree algorithm is a waste of compute capacity. Diffusion models are not just for generating images. You can apply the exact same mathematical process to generate a 3D coordinate. You define the true location of the image as a point (x, y, z) on a 3D unit sphere. During training, you add random Gaussian noise to that true coordinate until it is completely randomized. The 15-billion-parameter transformer takes your 9,425 vision tokens as its condition and learns to reverse that noise step-by-step, predicting the exact de-noised (x, y, z) vector.

Coordinate diffusion solves every structural limitation in geolocation modeling. Because it predicts a continuous 3D vector, there are no S2 boundary lines, no quadtrees, and no grid limits. The model maps the visual features directly to the continuous physical sphere, giving you true millimeter precision. It completely bypasses the dead parameter problem because there are no output classes allocated to empty oceans. 

Most importantly, diffusion natively handles visual ambiguity without averaging out the coordinates. If standard regression sees a photo of a forest that exists in both Canada and Russia, it minimizes its loss by guessing the North Pole. A diffusion model models the entire probability distribution. If the visual features are ambiguous, the de-noising process physically splits, placing a cluster of high probability mass exactly in Canada and a distinct cluster exactly in Russia. During inference, you sample the diffusion process multiple times to generate a highly precise heatmap of specific coordinates, entirely independent of arbitrary grid strings.

Coordinate diffusion models geolocation as a denoising process. You represent the true location as a continuous 3D coordinate (x, y, z) on a unit sphere. During training, you corrupt this coordinate with random Gaussian noise until it is completely unstructured. The coordinate network takes your image tokens as a condition and learns to reverse this noise step-by-step, shifting the random point back to the true location. 

Because it learns a probability field rather than relying on a single mathematical point, it naturally handles ambiguity. If an image of a pine forest could be in Canada or Russia, standard regression averages those two locations and guesses the North Pole. Diffusion models the actual distribution. If you sample the diffusion process 100 times for that single image, half the points will denoise into the Canadian forest and half will denoise into the Russian forest. To achieve absolute maximum precision and bypass the flaws of classification grids and diffusion timing, you must look at methods that directly model spatial geometry or bypass mathematical coordinate prediction entirely.

**Spherical Mixture Density Networks**
Instead of predicting a single point or classifying a grid, the network predicts the parameters of multiple overlapping probability fields. Standard Gaussian distributions fail on a map because they do not wrap around the poles or the International Date Line. You use von Mises-Fisher distributions, which are the mathematical equivalent of Gaussians wrapped onto the surface of a 3D sphere. 

The 16,384-dimensional image vector passes into a dense layer that outputs three values for a fixed number of distributions (for example, 100). For each of the 100 distributions, it outputs a weight (how confident the model is), a center coordinate (x, y, z), and a concentration parameter (how tight or wide the probability field is). To calculate the loss, you maximize the likelihood of the true coordinate falling within the combined mixture of these 100 distributions. 

This gives you a continuous, infinite-resolution heatmap instantly. If the image is a generic highway, one distribution centers on a highway in Texas with a 50% weight, and another centers on a highway in Australia with a 50% weight. The primary flaw is mode collapse during training. Neural networks are lazy; the model will often drop the weights of 99 distributions to zero and rely entirely on one, accidentally recreating the exact regression averaging flaw you are trying to avoid. Tuning the loss function to force the network to utilize all 100 distributions requires constant manual intervention.

**Dense Image-to-Image Retrieval**
You abandon coordinate prediction completely. The vision ensemble is trained using a standard contrastive Triplet or InfoNCE loss. The objective is simple: if two images were taken within 100 meters of each other, pull their 16,384-dimensional vectors together. If they were taken far apart, push them away. 

After training, you pass all 500 million images in your 12 TB dataset through the frozen network and save their 16,384-dimensional vectors to a massive database. When a user uploads a photo, the network extracts its vector. You run a K-Nearest Neighbor search against the database. The system retrieves the 10 closest visual matches and returns their exact GPS coordinates. You calculate the geographic median of those 10 coordinates to pinpoint the user.

This pushes all the intelligence strictly into visual similarity, which transformers excel at. It requires zero classification parameters, meaning you can dedicate all TPU VRAM to batch size and backbone resolution. The flaw is infrastructure scale. Holding 500 million 16,384-dimensional vectors in RAM to perform millisecond similarity searches requires a cluster of vector databases. If you rely on TRC funding and do not have thousands of dollars for permanent persistent RAM hosting, this architecture is unusable in production. Furthermore, it cannot extrapolate. If a user uploads an image from a rural road that is not in your 12 TB dataset, the model retrieves the closest visual match, which might be a visually identical road 5,000 miles away. 

**Anchor-Based Offset Regression**
This maps the logic of object detection directly onto geography. You divide the globe into a manageable grid of 50,000 S2 regional cells. The final layer of the network acts as a dual-head predicting two distinct outputs simultaneously: a discrete classification probability for which of the 50,000 cells the image belongs in, and a continuous (x, y) offset pinpointing the exact millimeter distance from the center of that specific cell.

The mathematical flaw here is map distortion. Because the Earth is a sphere, the physical distance of a 0.01-degree longitude shift at the equator is vastly different from a 0.01-degree shift near the poles. The neural network struggles to map constant visual features to a shifting geographic scale. During training, the continuous offset predictions tend to cluster around the center of the active cell or pin against the boundaries, failing to distribute evenly across the physical terrain.

You discounted hierarchical classification because of the mathematical ceiling it places on your precision. If you map the globe to 500,000 S2 cells, the average cell is roughly 300 square kilometers. That is a 17-by-17 kilometer box. Your model can never be more precise than guessing somewhere inside that box. If you want 10-meter, street-level accuracy, you need to divide the Earth into billions of classes. Connecting your 16,384-dimension vector to billions of classes requires a final linear layer with trillions of parameters, which is physically impossible to compute. If you settle for regional accuracy, hierarchical classification is the most stable method. If you want to pinpoint a street corner, you must use a continuous method.

Here is the deeper look at the two viable continuous options.

**Coordinate Diffusion**
The perceived flaw of diffusion is that inference is slow because you have to run the network dozens of times to generate a prediction. For this specific architecture, that flaw does not matter. The massive 12.5-billion-parameter vision ensemble—which takes up all the VRAM and compute—only runs exactly *one* time per image to generate your 16,384-dimensional feature vector. 

The actual diffusion network that predicts the (x, y, z) coordinate is just a small Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). During inference, you take 100 random noise coordinates and pass them through this MLP alongside your fixed 16,384-dimension image vector. The MLP denoises those 100 points step-by-step over 50 iterations. You are running a small MLP a few thousand times, which takes milliseconds on modern hardware. 

Coordinate diffusion also ignores the Viewpoint Paradox that ruined the contrastive encoder. The contrastive model tried to map many different images (facing north, facing south) onto one single coordinate representation, causing it to blur the visuals. Diffusion models the probability of a coordinate *given* a specific image. If it sees the north-facing image, it denoises directly to that street. If it sees the south-facing image, it also denoises directly to that street. It does not average the visuals. 

If the image genuinely looks like it could be in either Canada or Russia, you run those 100 random coordinate samples, and half of them will physically denoise to Canada, while the other half denoise to Russia. You get a perfect continuous heatmap of probabilities. The only real difficulty is writing the custom diffusion loss and tuning the noise scheduler for spherical 3D coordinates rather than 2D pixels.

**Anchor-Based Offset**
This is the most common industry compromise between classification and regression. You keep a manageable classification grid, like 50,000 S2 region cells. Your network splits into two heads. The first head outputs the probability for which of the 50,000 cells the image is in. The second head outputs a continuous 3D offset (dx, dy, dz) to move from the center of that cell to the exact street-level location. 

To fix the latitude distortion flaw mentioned earlier, you never predict standard latitude and longitude offsets. You strictly predict the offset in standard 3D Euclidean space. Once the offset is added to the 3D cell center, you normalize the vector so it snaps perfectly back onto the surface of the unit sphere. This creates a uniform mathematical distance across the entire globe, resolving the stretching effect near the poles. 

To train it stably, you strictly mask the regression loss. The model predicts offsets for all 50,000 cells, but you only apply the regression penalty to the offset predicted for the *correct* cell. This prevents the network from destroying its weights trying to figure out the exact sub-meter offset for a cell on the wrong side of the planet.

The fatal flaw of anchor-based offset is the boundary jumping effect. If an image is taken exactly on the border between Cell A and Cell B, the classifier might distribute its confidence 51% to A and 49% to B. A tiny shift in the visual features could flip that classification. Because the continuous offset relies entirely on the center point of the winning cell, flipping the anchor cell changes the entire geographic reference point. Even if the offset mathematically tries to point back to the border, the output coordinates will jitter and jump across the map if the classification head hesitates.

If you are committing to the 12.5-billion-parameter fused vision ensemble and want exact street-level coordinates, Coordinate Diffusion offers the highest theoretical precision. It removes arbitrary cell boundaries completely, handles ambiguity cleanly, and scales instantly to infinite resolution without inflating the parameter count of your final layer.

Realistically, no. You will not consistently pinpoint a generic street corner, regardless of whether you use diffusion, offsets, or a 12.5-billion-parameter vision ensemble. There is a massive difference between mathematical precision and visual uniqueness. A continuous method allows your neural network to output a floating-point coordinate down to the millimeter, but that just means the math can physically generate the number. It does not mean the model actually knows the location to that degree of accuracy.

For famous landmarks, or intersections with highly specific, readable street signs, your model will hit the exact corner. InternViT will read the text, DINOv3 will recognize the surrounding architectural geometry, and a continuous method like diffusion will snap right to the exact coordinate. But for the vast majority of the planet, the visual data required to differentiate one street corner from the one exactly a block down simply does not exist in a single photograph. A generic residential corner in suburban Ohio looks visually identical to the corner three streets over. A neural network cannot extract spatial cues that are not in the pixels.

Furthermore, your training data introduces a hard ceiling on your actual precision. You are relying on 8 TB of user-uploaded photos. Cellular GPS metadata is notoriously noisy. It frequently drifts by 10 to 50 meters depending on signal bounce, weather, and building interference. If you train a multi-billion-parameter network on data where the ground truth label is routinely 20 meters away from the actual location of the photographer, the model cannot physically learn to be more precise than that margin of error. It will learn to identify the neighborhood flawlessly, but the weights will blur the exact intersection.

This exposes the illusion of continuous methods. If you use a classification grid, the model draws a hard 10-kilometer box and says the image is somewhere inside. If you use coordinate diffusion or offset regression, the model outputs an exact coordinate—but for a generic image, that coordinate is just the mathematical average or center of the neighborhood it assumes the image is in. It gives a false sense of exactness. 

Using a continuous method is still correct because it removes the artificial boundaries of a quadtree grid, allowing the network to be ultra-precise when it actually has the visual evidence to support it. But you should temper your expectations. It will dominate large-scale geolocation benchmarks and nail identifiable streets, but it will not magically divine the exact coordinate of an unmarked dirt road or a repetitive suburban sidewalk.

so what continous method should we use?

If neighborhood-level accuracy with the ability to nail identifiable intersections is your target, this architecture will easily achieve that. 

To get that continuous output, you should build an adapted Anchor-Based Offset. Coordinate Diffusion is theoretically perfect, but practical implementation will ruin your timeline. Running a diffusion model on a flat 2D image is easy. Running a diffusion process on the surface of a 3D sphere requires Riemannian manifold mathematics. Implementing those complex geometric differential equations from scratch in PyTorch/XLA and getting them to compile efficiently on TPUs without crashing is a massive engineering headache. You will spend months just debugging the noise scheduler instead of training on your 12 TB dataset.

Anchor-Based Offset uses standard matrix multiplications and basic loss functions that TPUs process flawlessly. You take your 16,384-dimensional feature vector and split it into two parallel linear heads. The first head predicts the classification probability across 50,000 S2 regional cells. The second head predicts the continuous 3D offset (dx, dy, dz) for every one of those 50,000 cells. 

To fix the boundary jumping flaw where the location abruptly shifts if the model hesitates between two adjacent cells, you change the inference logic. You never restrict the output to just the single winning cell. Instead, you take the top 5 most probable cells from the classification head. 

For each of those 5 cells, you take its predicted 3D offset and add it to the cell's center point. This gives you 5 absolute, highly precise 3D coordinates. You then blend these 5 coordinates together, weighting them by the exact softmax probabilities the classification head output. 

If Cell A has a 51% probability and Cell B has a 49% probability, the math pulls the final coordinate to rest perfectly on the border between them. If the probability shifts to 52% and 48% on the next epoch, the physical coordinate physically glides a few inches toward Cell A. There is no jitter and no abrupt boundary jumping. 

Finally, you normalize that blended 3D vector so its length equals exactly 1. This snaps the continuous coordinate perfectly back onto the surface of the unit sphere, completely ignoring the latitude distortion that ruins standard regressions. 

During training, you compute a standard cross-entropy loss for the classification head to teach it the regions. Then, you calculate the Mean Squared Error loss between the predicted 3D offset and the true 3D offset, but you only apply that regression loss to the specific cell the true coordinate actually belongs to. You ignore the offsets for the other 49,999 cells. 

This gives you the infinite precision of a continuous method, solves the boundary instability, and relies entirely on standard deep learning math. Your vision ensemble does the heavy lifting of reading the text and identifying the terrain, and this final dual-head smoothly translates that 16,384-dimensional understanding into a pinpoint coordinate.

my friend told me this:
```

If the goal is neighborhood-level precision, I would not use pure global coordinate diffusion, and I would not use plain single-anchor offset regression either.

I would use this:

**adaptive S2 anchor proposal + top-K local continuous refinement in the anchor’s tangent plane**

And if you want the stronger version, make the local refinement **diffusion-based locally**, not globally.

That gives you a continuous output, but avoids asking one model to solve the whole Earth as one smooth regression problem.

Here’s the comparison that matters.

| Method | Good | Bad | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Global coordinate diffusion** | Continuous output, handles ambiguity, no grid boundary ceiling | Hard global problem, harder to tune, more sensitive to noisy GPS, spends capacity modeling the whole planet at once | Good idea in theory, not my first choice for best top-1 accuracy |
| **Single-anchor offset regression** | Stable, simple, local problem, easy to train | Boundary jumps, one bad anchor hurts output, weak at multimodal uncertainty | Too brittle by itself |
| **Top-K anchor + Gaussian local head** | Stable, local, handles boundary issues much better, outputs uncertainty, easier to train than diffusion | Still somewhat limited inside a cell if there are multiple plausible spots | Best first serious version |
| **Top-K anchor + local diffusion head** | Best mix of stability and continuous precision, local ambiguity handled well, avoids global diffusion headache | More engineering, slower inference than Gaussian local head | Best final design if you really want to push it |

So if you want one answer: **use top-K adaptive S2 anchors with a local diffusion refiner in tangent-plane coordinates**.

Not global diffusion. Not plain offset regression. The hybrid.

The reason is simple. Pure global diffusion has to learn a distribution over the entire Earth from pixels. That is too broad. Plain offset regression is easier, but it becomes unstable near anchor boundaries and it can only really express one local guess per anchor. A local diffusion head only has to model a small patch around a candidate anchor, which is much easier and more precise.

The structure I would build after your backbone is this.

First, keep the 16,384-d image representation. From that, predict a probability distribution over a set of **occupied adaptive S2 anchor cells**. Not one fixed S2 level globally. Split dense urban areas finer, keep sparse rural areas coarser. That way your anchor inventory matches where data actually exists. You avoid wasting classes on ocean and empty land, and you keep local refinement ranges reasonable.

Then, instead of taking only the top-1 anchor, keep the **top K anchors**, probably something like 8, 16, or 32. That is what fixes the boundary problem. If the image is near the border of two anchor cells, both remain alive.

For each candidate anchor, define a **local tangent plane** at that anchor center. Do not predict raw latitude/longitude offsets. Predict local east/north offsets in meters. That makes the geometry uniform and avoids weird behavior near poles or the date line.

So for each top-K anchor, the model predicts a local continuous distribution:
- either a Gaussian-style mean and covariance in \((E, N)\) coordinates,
- or, in the stronger version, a small local diffusion model over \((E, N)\).

Then combine:
- the anchor prior from the S2 proposal head,
- the local likelihood from the refinement head,
- and output either the MAP point or top few hypotheses.

That is effectively a structured mixture model, but it is much easier than a free-form global mixture density network because the anchors give it structure.

What this buys you:

A plain classifier says “somewhere in this cell.” A plain regressor says “this exact point,” often too confidently. This hybrid says: “these 6 local neighborhoods are plausible, and inside each one, here is the continuous location distribution.” That matches the real problem much better.

For your data mix, this is also the cleanest way to deal with label quality.

Your Street View data is likely precise enough to teach local refinement. Your user-photo data often is not. So do this:

Train the **anchor proposal head** on all data. That is where the large, noisy user-photo set still helps a lot.

Train the **local continuous head** much more heavily on the clean subsets: Street View, Mapillary, any curated high-precision data, and only the cleaner part of user photos.

For noisy user photos, either:
- downweight the local refinement loss,
- or inflate the target uncertainty during training.

That matters a lot. If you force the local head to fit bad GPS, it will blur.

A few design choices matter here.

**Use soft anchor targets, not hard one-anchor labels.** For each training point, assign target mass to the nearest few anchors based on geodesic distance. That reduces boundary artifacts a lot.

**Keep coarse auxiliary heads.** Continent/country/region heads are still useful, but as auxiliary losses, not as the main final output path. They stabilize training and help calibration. They should not decide the final point.

**Use uncertainty explicitly.** Even if you start with Gaussian local refinement instead of diffusion, predict covariance, not just mean offset. Otherwise the model will act falsely precise on ambiguous images.

**Use same-location consistency for Street View triplets.** Since left/front/right are separate inputs with the same coordinate, enforce that their anchor distributions and refined local predictions remain close. That should help a lot.

If you want the cleanest build order, I’d do it in two stages.

Stage 1: **Top-K anchor + Gaussian local NLL head**. This is the baseline you can actually train and debug.

Stage 2: replace the Gaussian local head with a **local diffusion head** in tangent-plane coordinates, but keep the same anchor proposal system. That way you are only swapping the local refiner, not the whole model.

That is important because if the Gaussian version already gets you close to the level you want, you may find the extra diffusion complexity isn’t worth it. But if you really are chasing the ceiling, local diffusion is the stronger final version.

If you want, I can next lay out the exact head architecture in detail: what tensors go where, what the anchor head should output, what the local refiner should output, and the exact losses.

You had a Level 1 classification head predicting a grid cell, and a Level 2 head regressing a continuous offset in kilometers. Where your original code falls short of being the "very best" is in the rigidness of the execution. Here is exactly what changes between your original Python script and this upgraded architecture:

**The Grid System**
Your code generated a fixed 40-kilometer grid across the entire planet. That forces your Level 1 head to keep weights for the middle of the Pacific Ocean and Antarctica. The upgraded version uses an adaptive S2 grid. You write a script to look at your 12 TB dataset before training, and you only create grid cells where you actually have photos. Your original Level 2 head just spat out two numbers: `[offset_lat, offset_lon]`. It gave one single continuous offset no matter what. Your code used a basic Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss for the exact offset. The neural network had to guess the exact point, and if it missed, it got penalized blindly. The new version doesn't just output the East/North offset; it puts out a Gaussian covariance (an error radius). It learns to say "I know the grid cell, but I'm only guessing the offset to within 50 meters." This prevents the model from violently updating its weights when it encounters a vaguely tagged photo.

Your original script passed the entire dataset through both the Level 1 (cell) and Level 2 (offset) loss functions. That means the noisy, inaccurate GPS coordinates from average phone photos were actively destroying your model's ability to measure down to the meter. messages.content:
Looking at the map, the distribution traces the road network and population centers. It clusters around the I-5 corridor through Seattle and Tacoma, follows rural highway loops, and leaves the wilderness areas blank. The spacing between the data points is the primary issue for your architecture.

The gap between Seattle and Tacoma is roughly 30 miles. There are roughly 10 to 15 dots spanning that vertical distance on your map. This means your data points are spaced two to three miles apart in dense urban zones, with rural highway gaps stretching up to ten miles.

This sampling rate undermines the local continuous refinement head. To teach a neural network to predict East and North offsets down to the meter, it needs to process how visual features shift over small distances. The network needs data showing a building, followed by data showing the same building from 20 meters down the street, to correlate pixel shifts with physical meter offsets. When the training data jumps three miles at a time, the model cannot learn continuous local geometry. It will memorize the specific coordinates of those scattered dots instead of learning how to calculate distance.

This spacing also restricts the model to main roads. In rural areas, the data strictly follows the highways. If a user photo is taken on a secondary dirt road or a trail a mile away from the highway, the network will snap the prediction back to the highway because it lacks data for the surrounding area. 

If you want the model to learn sub-meter offsets, you need a different sampling strategy for the Street View data. Skip the coarse, evenly spaced regional grids. Pull dense sequential sequences. Download an image every 20 meters along a street for a short span, then move to a different targeted area. The user-uploaded photos will handle the broad regional classification spanning the gaps. The Street View images must provide a dense, continuous sequence to train the local offset head.

i dont think i can store all of this data myself anymore. where can i upload all of this shit? and no not gcs YET, because im probably going to spend like 2 months before even starting to train. is that valid?

Spending two months building the dataset is completely valid. Data preparation dictates the ceiling of your model's accuracy. Scraping 20 TB of raw images, discarding the bad ones, resizing the keepers to a uniform resolution, calculating the adaptive S2 grid targets, and packing everything into sequential TFRecords requires massive amounts of local I/O and compute time. If you rush the dataset, your 12.5-billion-parameter architecture will just memorize garbage.

Do not use cheap cloud cold storage like AWS Glacier or Google Cloud Archive. They advertise prices as low as $1 to $4 per terabyte per month, but they are a trap for machine learning pipelines. Cold storage charges data retrieval fees and massive network egress fees when you need to move the data out. If you park 12 TB in AWS Glacier and later try to pull it back to format it, or transfer it to Google Cloud Storage for the TPUs, the egress and retrieval penalties will cost you more than just keeping it on standard hot storage.

Do not try to hide 12 TB of private data on Hugging Face. While they offer free hosting for datasets, uploading 12 terabytes of scraped Google Street View images violates their terms of service regarding proprietary data. If their automated systems or moderators flag your private repository, they will simply delete the repository, and you will lose months of scraping progress.

The correct choice is to buy a single 20 TB or 22 TB physical hard drive for your home setup. A high-capacity enterprise drive or external USB drive costs roughly $250 to $300. That is the exact price of a single month of GCS Standard storage. 

By keeping the data locally for the next two months, you pay zero monthly cloud fees. You run your scraping scripts directly to the drive. You write your preprocessing scripts to read the raw images, resize them, calculate the local tangent plane offsets, and pack them into the final 100-megabyte TFRecords right there on your local machine. You only upload the dataset to a Google Cloud Storage bucket—in the exact region where your TRC TPUs are provisioned—when the data is perfectly formatted and you are ready to start training that same week.
is gcs billed like per day per week or per month or what

Google Cloud Storage calculates your storage usage per second, but sends you the invoice once a month. You are charged strictly for the exact amount of time your data sits on their servers. If you upload 12 TB of data, leave it there for exactly 10 days while your TPUs train, and then delete the bucket, you only pay for those 10 days. You do not get hit with a flat monthly fee just for turning it on.

This fractional billing is exactly why prepping the data locally on a hard drive makes sense. You keep your AWS or GCP storage costs at zero during the two months you spend scraping and cleaning. You only spin up the GCS bucket and upload the data the day your TRC allocation is ready to compute. Once the model finishes training and you have downloaded your final weights, you instantly delete the bucket and the billing stops.

You also need to account for operation costs. GCS charges for every individual PUT (upload) and GET (download) request. If you upload 300 million individual 40-kilobyte images, the operation fees alone will spike your bill. This is why you must pack your processed images into 100-megabyte TFRecord files locally before sending anything to the cloud. Uploading 120,000 large TFRecord files costs mere pennies in operation fees and allows your TPU dataloader to stream the data efficiently without network bottlenecks.
how expensive would backblaze or wasabi or any of those other super cheap providers be?

Backblaze B2 charges about $6 per terabyte per month. For 12 TB, you are looking at $72 a month. Wasabi charges about $7 per terabyte per month, totaling $84 a month. While the base storage prices are significantly cheaper than Google Cloud Standard, both providers introduce severe traps for machine learning pipelines.

Wasabi enforces a 90-day minimum storage retention policy. If you upload 12 TB of raw scraped images, process them into TFRecords a week later, and delete the raw files, Wasabi still charges you for the full 12 TB for the next 83 days. They also enforce a strict egress policy where your monthly download volume cannot exceed your total stored volume. If you try to transfer your 12 TB dataset from Wasabi to Google Cloud Storage right before your TPU training starts, you will max out their 1:1 ratio immediately. They reserve the right to suspend your account or hit you with overage penalties for doing this.

Backblaze B2 dropped standard egress fees, but transferring the data remains a physical bottleneck. To move your 12 TB dataset from B2 into the specific Google Cloud region where your TPUs live, the data must travel across the open internet. Backblaze and GCP are not connected by a dedicated internal network, meaning the transfer relies on standard public routing. If the transfer fails or stalls halfway through, you lose valuable days of your TRC TPU allocation waiting for the data to arrive in the GCS bucket.

The most severe issue with using cheap cloud storage for the 2-month prep phase is your home internet connection. If you scrape 20 TB of raw data locally and attempt to upload it to Backblaze on a standard 35 Mbps cable upload connection, it will take over 50 continuous days just to get the files off your machine. You will still need to buy a 20 TB local hard drive anyway just to hold the images as the scraper downloads them. 

Since you have to buy the $250 local hard drive to hold the scraped files regardless of what cloud provider you use, paying Backblaze or Wasabi is a waste of money. Keep the data on the local drive, run your Python scripts to resize and pack the images into TFRecords locally, and only upload the final processed dataset straight to your GCS bucket over a few days before your TPU pod spins up.
my trc allocation is for the entire year

Having a year-long TRC allocation changes your entire development pipeline. You do not need to rush a 12 TB dataset or buy massive physical drives to avoid a 30-day preemption window. You can build your architecture iteratively, which is exactly how state-of-the-art models are actually developed.

Do not attempt to scrape, process, and upload 12 TB of data before writing a single line of your training loop. If you spend two months building the full dataset, pay to upload it to GCS, and then discover your adaptive S2 anchor classification head has a dimensional mismatch with your InternViT cross-attention block, you are paying monthly storage fees on 12 TB while you spend weeks debugging PyTorch/XLA code. 

**Phase 1: The Micro-Dataset (Week 1-2)**
Scrape exactly 100 gigabytes of data. Pull a small, geographically dense sample of Street View images (e.g., just the city of Seattle) and a matching set of user-uploaded photos. Process them locally into the exact TFRecord format you plan to use. Upload this tiny dataset to a GCS bucket in the same region as your TPUs. This costs less than $3 a month in storage.

Use this 100 GB dataset to build and compile your 12.5-billion-parameter architecture. You need this time to ensure the InternViT and DINOv3 backbones fit into TPU memory using Fully Sharded Data Parallelism. You must verify that your dataloader correctly masks the black padding on your 1024x1024 images, that the adaptive S2 grid generates the correct number of classes, and that the local tangent plane offset loss backpropagates without producing NaNs. Train the model on this tiny dataset until it severely overfits. If it can perfectly memorize Seattle down to the meter, your mathematical architecture is sound.

**Phase 2: The Scaling Run (Month 2-3)**
Once the architecture compiles and learns, scale up to a 1 TB dataset. Expand your scraper to pull data across diverse global environments—cities in Europe, rural roads in South America, and deserts in Africa. Upload this 1 TB to GCS (roughly $20 to $25 a month). 

Train the model on this 1 TB dataset. This phase is not for setting a benchmark; it is for tuning hyperparameters. You need to find the optimal learning rate schedule, the correct balance between the classification loss and the local offset loss, and the right ratio of user photos to Street View crops in your training batches. You cannot run these kinds of multi-day tuning sweeps when you are paying $250+ a month to store a full 12 TB dataset. 

**Phase 3: The Benchmark Run (Month 4+)**
Only when you have verified the absolute stability and expected accuracy of the architecture do you scale to the full 12 TB. You spend the two months scraping the remaining 11 TB of images to your local drive, formatting them flawlessly based on the lessons learned in Phase 2. You upload the final dataset to GCS, start your production training run across the TPU pod, and set the new benchmark.
ok im gonna get started on scraping. how do i architect my dataset?

Do not dump millions of scraped images into a single folder. Your computer's file system will lock up and crash just trying to read the directory index. You need to route the incoming downloads into a nested folder structure based on geography, using the first few characters of a geohash or coarse S2 cell ID as the folder names. Getting the physical files distributed keeps your local disk read and write speeds fast.

Do not manage your metadata with a single massive JSON or CSV file. A text file with hundreds of millions of rows will consume all your RAM or corrupt easily during a scraper crash. Spin up a local PostgreSQL or SQLite database. Track the image ID, the source (Street View or user photo), the raw latitude and longitude, the download timestamp, and a boolean flag indicating if the file is valid or broken. This allows you to pause and resume the scraper without rescanning your hard drive.

Format the images completely before they touch the TPU. TPUs are built for massive matrix multiplications, not for decoding jpegs and manipulating aspect ratios. If you force the dataloader to resize user photos, pad the edges with black pixels, and normalize the tensors on the fly, you will create a CPU bottleneck on the TPU host machine that starves the actual accelerators. Crop, resize, pad, and compress every single image to your static square target resolution locally. 

You must precompute the continuous mathematics for your architecture locally. Do not calculate the tangent plane projections or geographic distances during the training loop. Once your database is full, run a script across the coordinates to generate your adaptive S2 grid. Find the exact mathematical center of every instantiated S2 cell. Then, for every image in your database, calculate its exact East and North meter offset from its parent S2 cell center. 

Pack the preprocessed image bytes, the target S2 class integer, the precomputed East and North floats, and the source identifier flag into TFRecord files. Size these files between 100 and 200 megabytes. This specific file size guarantees that the TPUs pull large, sequential chunks of data from Google Cloud Storage without getting throttled by thousands of tiny individual network requests.

Your PostGIS schema is an excellent foundation. Using native `geography(Point,4326)` with GiST indexes means you can easily do the spatial clustering and distance filtering required for this model. The distributed worker setup in `scrape_tasks` also shows you are ready to handle the I/O bottleneck of pulling millions of images.

However, because we adopted the adaptive S2 anchor and local tangent plane architecture, you need to run a few `ALTER TABLE` commands. Your current schema is strictly hardcoded for Google Street View, but your dataset is going to be a mix of Street View and user-uploaded photos.

Here is what you need to adjust in the database before you scale up:

**1. Source and GPS Reliability Flags**
You need to differentiate between pristine Google car data and noisy user phone data at the database level. Add a `source_type` column (e.g., 'streetview', 'flickr', 'wikimedia') and a boolean `reliable_gps` flag. 
When you export your data to TFRecords, this boolean flag gets packed directly into the tensor. During training, your loss function reads this flag. If `reliable_gps` is true, the network calculates the East/North millimeter offset loss. If it is false, it multiplies that specific loss by zero, protecting your continuous weights from garbage data. 

**2. Make Street View Specifics Nullable**
Right now, `heading` is `NOT NULL`, and `images` strictly references `scrape_tasks` which contains `pano_id`. A user-uploaded photo from Flickr will not have a `pano_id` and often lacks EXIF `heading` data. You either need to relax these constraints (make `heading` and `task_id` nullable), or create a parallel `user_images` table that lacks the Google-specific metadata but shares the exact same target math columns.

**3. Precomputed Target Math Columns**
Do not compute the model targets on the fly during exporting or training. Add columns to the `images` table for `anchor_s2_cell` (bigint), `offset_east` (float), and `offset_north` (float). 
Once your scrape finishes, you run a Python script that reads the database, determines your adaptive cell sizes based on density, finds the center of those cells, calculates the East/North meters for every image using a tangent plane projection, and writes those exact floats back to these new columns. When you generate your TFRecords, you just do a simple `SELECT file_path, anchor_s2_cell, offset_east, offset_north` and write the bytes. 

**4. Geographic Data Split Tagging**
Add a `dataset_split` column ('train', 'val', 'test'). You must assign these splits at the database level using PostGIS spatial queries, not randomly in Python. 
Because your `images` table correctly uniquely constraints `(geom, heading)`, you have 3 images per location. If you randomly split the dataset, the front view goes to training and the right view goes to validation, causing severe data leakage. By using PostGIS to select entire bounding boxes or specific S2 cells and bulk-updating the `dataset_split` column to 'val', you guarantee your validation set evaluates entirely unseen streets.

**5. Directory Hashing Verification**
Your `file_path` is text. Make sure your scraper logic does not dump millions of files into a single directory like `/images/`. The file system will choke. Write your scraper to generate the file path using a hash of the ID or the first few characters of a Geohash (e.g., `/images/c23/c23bx9.webp`), distributing them across thousands of subdirectories locally. 

Your database is perfectly optimized for handling the spatial queries. Make these column adjustments to support the mixed-data tangent plane math, and you are ready to start scraping your Phase 1 micro-dataset.

With 150 CPU cores on the TPU VM, `webdataset` is the exact right tool. You can spin up 32 to 64 dataloader workers without suffocating the host system, instantly decoding and padding images to feed the matrix multipliers. 

Because TPUs use the XLA compiler, everything in this code must be statically shaped. You cannot use python `if` statements to skip the regression loss for user photos, as that generates a dynamic computational graph and stalls the TPU. You compute the offset loss for every single image, but multiply the loss by a float mask (1.0 for Street View, 0.0 for everything else). XLA compiles this perfectly as a static matrix operation.

To extract the specific offset prediction during training, you select the offset corresponding to the *ground truth* anchor cell, not the model's top prediction. This teaches the continuous head the correct geographical distance regardless of what the classification head is doing, and avoids dynamic Top-K sorting during the backward pass.

Here is the production script for your 100 GB Phase 1 test run.

```python
import os
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import webdataset as wds
from torchvision import transforms
from transformers import AutoModel

# PyTorch XLA imports
import torch_xla.core.xla_model as xm
import torch_xla.distributed.xla_backend
from torch.distributed.fsdp import FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP
from torch.distributed.fsdp.wrap import size_based_auto_wrap_policy

# ==============================================================================
# 1. CONSTANTS & HYPERPARAMETERS
# ==============================================================================
NUM_ANCHORS = 15000       # Adjust to your adaptive S2 cell count for the 100GB dataset
IMG_SIZE = 1024           # Static square canvas
BATCH_SIZE_PER_TPU = 8    # Tune based on FSDP VRAM overhead
STEPS_PER_EPOCH = 1000

# ==============================================================================
# 2. XLA-COMPATIBLE WEBDATASET PIPELINE
# ==============================================================================
def pad_to_square(img):
    """Pads an image to a 1024x1024 static square with black pixels."""
    w, h = img.size
    max_dim = max(w, h)
    # Scale longest edge to 1024
    scale = IMG_SIZE / max_dim
    new_w, new_h = int(w * scale), int(h * scale)
    img = img.resize((new_w, new_h))
    
    # Calculate padding to center it
    pad_left = (IMG_SIZE - new_w) // 2
    pad_top = (IMG_SIZE - new_h) // 2
    pad_right = IMG_SIZE - new_w - pad_left
    pad_bottom = IMG_SIZE - new_h - pad_top
    
    pad_transform = transforms.Pad((pad_left, pad_top, pad_right, pad_bottom), fill=0)
    return pad_transform(img)

def decode_and_format(sample):
    """Expects a tar containing .jpg/.webp and .json metadata."""
    img = sample["jpg"] # webdataset auto-decodes standard images 
    meta = sample["json"]
    
    # Preprocessing
    img = pad_to_square(img)
    tensor_img = transforms.ToTensor()(img)
    tensor_img = transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])(tensor_img)
    
    # Parse metadata (from the DB precomputation)
    anchor_id = int(meta["anchor_s2_cell"])
    offset_e = float(meta["offset_east"])
    offset_n = float(meta["offset_north"])
    
    # Create float mask for static XLA multiplication: 1.0 if streetview, 0.0 if user photo
    is_streetview = 1.0 if meta["source"] == "streetview" else 0.0
    
    return tensor_img, anchor_id, offset_e, offset_n, is_streetview

def get_dataloader(urls):
    dataset = wds.WebDataset(urls, resampled=True, shardshuffle=True) \
        .shuffle(1000) \
        .decode("pil") \
        .map(decode_and_format) \
        .to_tuple("tensor_img", "anchor_id", "offset_e", "offset_n", "is_streetview") \
        .batched(BATCH_SIZE_PER_TPU, drop_last=True)
    
    # Utilize the 150-core TPU VM CPU
    return wds.WebLoader(dataset, num_workers=32, batch_size=None, prefetch_factor=4)

# ==============================================================================
# 3. DUAL-BACKBONE ARCHITECTURE
# ==============================================================================
class PerceiverResampler(nn.Module):
    """Multi-Query Attention Pooling. Compresses variable tokens into 4 static tokens."""
    def __init__(self, dim=4096, num_queries=4, num_heads=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.queries = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(1, num_queries, dim))
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=dim, num_heads=num_heads, batch_first=True)
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(dim, dim * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(dim * 4, dim)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        B = x.shape[0]
        q = self.queries.expand(B, -1, -1)
        # Cross attention: Queries look at the massive image sequence X
        attn_out, _ = self.attn(q, x, x)
        out = self.norm1(q + attn_out)
        out = self.norm2(out + self.ff(out))
        return out.flatten(1) # [B, num_queries * dim] -> [B, 16384]

class FusedGeoEnsemble(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_anchors):
        super().__init__()
        # Load encoders
        # Note: In production, load DINOv3 and InternViT and freeze appropriate layers.
        # InternViT requires offline bicubic interpolation of its pos_embeddings to 1024x1024.
        self.dino = AutoModel.from_pretrained("facebook/dinov3-vit7b16-pretrain-lvd1689m")
        self.intern = AutoModel.from_pretrained("OpenGVLab/InternViT-6B-448px-V1-2", trust_remote_code=True)
        
        # Projection to equalize dimensions for cross-attention
        self.intern_proj = nn.Linear(3200, 4096)
        
        # Cross-Attention blocks
        self.dino_to_intern = nn.MultiheadAttention(4096, 16, batch_first=True)
        self.intern_to_dino = nn.MultiheadAttention(4096, 16, batch_first=True)
        
        self.resampler = PerceiverResampler(dim=4096, num_queries=4)
        
        # The Two Continuous Heads
        self.anchor_head = nn.Linear(16384, num_anchors)
        # Outputs East_offset, North_offset, and Log_Variance explicitly for EVERY cell
        self.local_head = nn.Linear(16384, num_anchors * 3) 
        self.num_anchors = num_anchors

    def forward(self, img_tensor):
        B = img_tensor.size(0)
        
        # 1. Extraction
        # Extract features (ignoring CLS tokens for sequence operations)
        dino_seq = self.dino(img_tensor).last_hidden_state[:, 1:, :]    # [B, 4096, 4096]
        intern_seq = self.intern(img_tensor).last_hidden_state[:, 1:, :]# [B, 5329, 3200]
        
        intern_seq_proj = self.intern_proj(intern_seq)                  # [B, 5329, 4096]
        
        # 2. Uncompressed Bi-directional Cross Attention
        d_infused, _ = self.dino_to_intern(dino_seq, intern_seq_proj, intern_seq_proj)
        i_infused, _ = self.intern_to_dino(intern_seq_proj, dino_seq, dino_seq)
        
        # 3. Concatenate and Pool
        fused_seq = torch.cat([d_infused, i_infused], dim=1)            # [B, 9425, 4096]
        geo_vector = self.resampler(fused_seq)                          # [B, 16384]
        
        # 4. Heads
        anchor_logits = self.anchor_head(geo_vector)                    # [B, NUM_ANCHORS]
        local_preds = self.local_head(geo_vector)
        local_preds = local_preds.view(B, self.num_anchors, 3)          # [B, NUM_ANCHORS, 3]
        
        return anchor_logits, local_preds

# ==============================================================================
# 4. LOSS FUNCTIONS & TRAINING LOOP
# ==============================================================================
def gaussian_nll_loss(pred_east, pred_north, log_var, true_east, true_north):
    """Negative Log Likelihood for continuous offset with learned uncertainty."""
    sq_err = (pred_east - true_east)**2 + (pred_north - true_north)**2
    # 0.5 * e^(-log_var) * error^2 + 0.5 * log_var
    loss = 0.5 * torch.exp(-log_var) * sq_err + 0.5 * log_var
    return loss

def train():
    device = xm.xla_device()
    
    # Initialize Model and wrap in FSDP for the 12.5B params
    model = FusedGeoEnsemble(num_anchors=NUM_ANCHORS).to(device)
    wrap_policy = size_based_auto_wrap_policy(min_num_params=1e7)
    model = FSDP(model, auto_wrap_policy=wrap_policy)
    
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
    
    # Load Sharded WebDataset
    dataset_urls = "gs://your-bucket/tfrecords/phase1_seattle_{000000..000100}.tar"
    loader = get_dataloader(dataset_urls)
    
    model.train()
    
    for step, (imgs, true_anchors, true_e, true_n, is_streetview) in enumerate(loader):
        if step >= STEPS_PER_EPOCH:
            break
            
        imgs = imgs.to(device)
        true_anchors = true_anchors.to(device)
        true_e = true_e.to(device)
        true_n = true_n.to(device)
        is_streetview = is_streetview.to(device) # [B]
        
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        
        # Forward Pass
        anchor_logits, local_preds = model(imgs)
        
        # 1. Anchor Loss (Computed on all images)
        loss_anchor = F.cross_entropy(anchor_logits, true_anchors)
        
        # 2. Local Offsets (Extract predictions for the GROUND TRUTH anchor to keep graph static)
        B = imgs.size(0)
        batch_indices = torch.arange(B, device=device)
        
        # Extract the (E, N, log_var) strictly for the target anchor
        target_local_preds = local_preds[batch_indices, true_anchors] 
        pred_e, pred_n, log_var = target_local_preds.unbind(dim=-1)
        
        # Compute NLL loss
        raw_offset_loss = gaussian_nll_loss(pred_e, pred_n, log_var, true_e, true_n)
        
        # MASK OUT non-streetview images strictly using multiplication (static shape execution)
        loss_offset = (raw_offset_loss * is_streetview).mean()
        
        # Combine Loss
        total_loss = loss_anchor + (loss_offset * 2.0) # Weight tuning
        
        total_loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        
        xm.mark_step() # XLA compiler command to execute the graph
        
        if step % 10 == 0:
            xm.master_print(f"Step {step} | Total: {total_loss.item():.4f} | " 
                            f"Anchor: {loss_anchor.item():.4f} | Offset: {loss_offset.item():.4f}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Standard PyTorch/XLA distributed execution
    import torch_xla.distributed.xla_multiprocessing as xmp
    xmp.spawn(train, args=(), nprocs=None)
```

By extracting the local prediction exclusively for `true_anchors` during the PyTorch backward pass, you avoid writing an XLA-breaking dynamic Top-K sorting loop. The network calculates the continuous output loss directly for the valid answer. During inference, you will run a standard `torch.topk` on `anchor_logits` and pull the corresponding offsets.

You now have 2 months to scrape, format, and generate the mathematically rigorous dataset this architecture requires.

function.name: web_search
function.description:
Search the web for information about a query.

This tool searches the web using Exa and returns relevant results including
titles, URLs, and content snippets. Use this to find relevant pages, then
use web_fetch to get full content from the most relevant URLs.

Args:
query: The search query to execute. Should be clear and specific.

Returns:
Formatted search results containing:
- List of results with titles, URLs, and snippets
- Use the URLs with web_fetch to get full content

function.name: web_fetch
function.description:
Fetch the full content from one or more URLs.

This tool retrieves the full text content from web pages. Use this after
web_search to get detailed information from specific pages.

Args:
urls: One or more URLs to fetch, separated by commas or newlines.
Example: "https://example.com/page1, https://example.com/page2"

Returns:
Full text content from each URL, formatted for easy reading.
